title: Continuous-data-assimilation-enabled fast and robust convergence of an Uzawa-based solver for Navier-Stokes equations with large Reynolds number
summary: This paper shows how continuous data assimilation (CDA) can be used to provably enable and accelerate convergence of a (efficient at each iteration due to a physics-splitting, but generally slowly converging and not robust) nonlinear solver for incompressible Navier-Stokes equations (NSE). Herein we develop, analyze and test an Uzawa-based nonlinear solver for incompressible NSE that incorporates partial solution data into the iteration through continuous data assimilation (CDA-Uzawa). We rigorously prove that i) CDA-Uzawa will accelerate a converging Uzawa iteration, and more partial solution data yields more acceleration, and ii) with enough partial solution data CDA-Uzawa will converge for arbitrarily large Reynolds numbers, even if multiple NSE solutions exist. In the case of noisy data, we prove that the convergence results hold down to the size of the noise, and we propose a strategy to pass to Newton once CDA-Uzawa convergence reaches its lower limit. Results of several numerical tests illustrate the theory and show CDA-Uzawa is a very effective and efficient solver. While this paper focuses a particular splitting-based solver for the NSE, the key ideas are quite general and extendable to a wide class of nonlinear solvers.

AI.tldr: CDA-Uzawa solver combines continuous data assimilation with Uzawa iteration to accelerate convergence and ensure robustness for incompressible Navier-Stokes equations, even at high Reynolds numbers and with noisy data.
AI.motivation: Standard Uzawa-based nonlinear solvers for incompressible Navier-Stokes equations are efficient per iteration due to physics-splitting but converge slowly and lack robustness, especially at high Reynolds numbers where multiple solutions may exist.
AI.method: Develops CDA-Uzawa method that incorporates partial solution data into Uzawa iteration through continuous data assimilation. The approach uses available partial data to guide the solver, with theoretical analysis proving convergence properties and acceleration effects.
AI.result: Theoretically proves that: 1) CDA-Uzawa accelerates converging Uzawa iterations, with more data yielding more acceleration; 2) With sufficient partial data, CDA-Uzawa converges for arbitrarily large Reynolds numbers even with multiple NSE solutions; 3) With noisy data, convergence holds down to noise level, and proposes switching to Newton method once CDA-Uzawa reaches its lower limit. Numerical tests confirm effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: CDA-Uzawa is an effective and efficient solver for incompressible Navier-Stokes equations that leverages partial data to overcome limitations of traditional Uzawa methods. The approach generalizes to other nonlinear solvers beyond the specific NSE application.

title: Matrix-Free Stabilized BDF Schemes for Semilinear Parabolic Equations with Unconditional Maximum Bound Principle Preservation and Energy Stability
summary: We develop a family of stabilized backward differentiation formula (sBDF) schemes of orders one through four for semilinear parabolic equations. The proposed methods are designed to achieve three properties that are rarely available simultaneously in high-order time discretizations: unconditional preservation of the maximum bound principle (MBP), unconditional discrete energy stability, and practical matrix-free implementation. The construction integrates carefully designed stabilization terms, fixed-point iterations, and a pointwise cut-off strategy. The nonlinear algebraic systems arising from the implicit sBDF discretizations are solved by fixed-point iteration, resulting in fully matrix-free algorithms. This makes the approach particularly attractive for practical computations on general domains and under mixed boundary conditions, where FFT-based exponential time differencing methods are often unavailable or inefficient. We further present a unified analysis for the fully implemented schemes, explicitly incorporating the interplay among time discretization, nonlinear iteration, and cut-off. Unconditional contractivity of the fixed-point iterations and error estimates are established. For the Allen-Cahn equation, we additionally prove an unconditional discrete energy dissipation law. Numerical experiments confirm the theoretical convergence rates and demonstrate the robustness and efficiency of the proposed methods, particularly relative to ETD-based approaches for problems with mixed boundary conditions.

AI.tldr: A family of stabilized BDF schemes (orders 1-4) for semilinear parabolic equations that simultaneously achieve unconditional MBP preservation, unconditional energy stability, and matrix-free implementation.
AI.motivation: To develop high-order time discretizations that combine three rarely available properties simultaneously: unconditional maximum bound principle preservation, unconditional discrete energy stability, and practical matrix-free implementation, particularly for problems where FFT-based methods are unavailable or inefficient.
AI.method: Stabilized BDF schemes with carefully designed stabilization terms, fixed-point iterations, and pointwise cut-off strategy. Nonlinear systems are solved via fixed-point iteration, resulting in fully matrix-free algorithms.
AI.result: Unconditional contractivity of fixed-point iterations and error estimates are established. For Allen-Cahn equation, unconditional discrete energy dissipation law is proved. Numerical experiments confirm theoretical convergence rates and demonstrate robustness/efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed sBDF schemes successfully achieve the three desired properties simultaneously, offering an attractive alternative to ETD-based approaches, especially for problems with mixed boundary conditions on general domains.

title: Data-dependent approximation through RBF
summary:
In this article we present a modification of classical Radial Basis Function (RBF) interpolation techniques aimed at reducing oscillations near discontinuities in one and two dimensions. Our approach introduces an adaptive mechanism by varying the shape parameter of the RBFs and making it data-dependent, forcing it to tend to infinity in the vicinity of discontinuities. This modification results in kernel functions that locally resemble %Kronecker delta functions, effectively minimizing spurious oscillations.
  To detect discontinuities, we employ smoothness indicators: for grid-based data, these are computed as undivided second-order differences squared. For scattered data, we use least squares approximations of the Laplacian multiplied by the square of the mean local separation of the stencil points, and then squared. These indicators guide the adaptive adjustment of the shape parameter.
  We prove the invertibility of the resulting interpolation matrix and propose a solution strategy that maintains the condition number comparable to that of a system where points near discontinuities are excluded. Numerical experiments in one and two dimensions demonstrate that the proposed method significantly reduces oscillations near discontinuities across various kernel types, whether locally or globally supported. At the same time, the interpolation accuracy and matrix conditioning in smooth regions remain essentially unchanged, as measured by the infinity norm of the error and the condition number.

AI.tldr: Modified RBF interpolation with adaptive shape parameter that approaches infinity near discontinuities, reducing oscillations while maintaining accuracy in smooth regions.
AI.motivation: Classical RBF interpolation suffers from spurious oscillations near discontinuities, which degrades solution quality. The authors aim to develop an adaptive RBF method that can handle discontinuities without sacrificing accuracy in smooth regions.
AI.method: Introduces data-dependent adaptive shape parameter for RBFs that tends to infinity near discontinuities, making kernels locally resemble Kronecker delta functions. Uses smoothness indicators (undivided second-order differences squared for grid data, Laplacian approximations for scattered data) to detect discontinuities. Proves invertibility of interpolation matrix and develops solution strategy to maintain reasonable condition numbers.
AI.result: Numerical experiments in 1D and 2D show significant reduction of oscillations near discontinuities across various kernel types (locally/globally supported). Interpolation accuracy and matrix conditioning in smooth regions remain essentially unchanged as measured by infinity norm error and condition number.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive RBF method successfully reduces oscillations near discontinuities while preserving accuracy in smooth regions, with proven mathematical properties and practical implementation strategies for both grid-based and scattered data.

title: A Systematic Framework for Stable and Cost-Efficient Matrix Polynomial Evaluation
summary:
A method for evaluating matrix polynomials have recently been developed that require one fewer matrix product ($1M$) than the Paterson--Stockmeyer (PS) method. Since the computational cost for large-scale matrices is asymptotically determined by the number of matrix products, this reduction directly affects the total execution time. However, the coefficients in these optimized formulas emerge as solutions to systems of nonlinear polynomial equations, resulting in multiple potential solution sets. An inappropriate selection of these coefficients can lead to numerical instability in floating-point arithmetic.
  This paper presents a systematic framework and a MATLAB implementation, MatrixPolEval1, used to obtain and validate stable coefficient sets for matrix polynomials of degrees $m \in \{8, 10, 12\}$ and above. The framework introduces structural variants to maintain stability even when the original configuration fails to yield a robust solution. The provided tool identifies stable coefficient sets using variable precision arithmetic (VPA) and provides a reliability indicator for expected accuracy. Numerical experiments on polynomials arising in applications, including the matrix exponential and geometric series, show that the framework achieves the $1M$ saving while maintaining numerical accuracy comparable to the PS method.

AI.tldr: A framework and MATLAB tool (MatrixPolEval1) for finding stable coefficient sets for matrix polynomial evaluation that saves one matrix product compared to Paterson-Stockmeyer method, with validation for degrees 8, 10, 12+.
AI.motivation: Recent methods reduce matrix polynomial evaluation cost by one matrix product compared to PS method, but coefficient solutions from nonlinear equations can cause numerical instability if chosen poorly.
AI.method: Systematic framework with MATLAB implementation MatrixPolEval1 to obtain and validate stable coefficient sets using variable precision arithmetic (VPA), with structural variants for robustness when original configurations fail.
AI.result: Tool identifies stable coefficient sets with reliability indicators; numerical experiments on matrix exponential and geometric series show 1M saving while maintaining accuracy comparable to PS method.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully achieves computational savings of one matrix product while preserving numerical stability for matrix polynomial evaluation.

title: Korn's inequality from the viewpoint of calculus of variations
summary: We study the best possible constants in Korn-type inequalities and their connection with Morrey's problem in the calculus of variations. We adapt techniques from the analysis of the Beurling-Ahlfors transform to Korn's inequality. In particular we show that the constant in Korn's inequality admits a dimension-free bound, and we obtain an estimate that is sharp up to a factor of $\sqrt 3$. In the radial case, the estimate is sharp. We also establish several improvements to estimates in various function spaces. Using a weighted version of Burkholder's differential subordination theorem, recently introduced in [J. Reine Angew. Math. 824 (2025), pp. 137-166],we also prove a dimension-free weighted version of the inequality for Muckenhoupt weights.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes dimension-free bounds for Korn-type inequalities, connecting them to Morrey's problem and adapting techniques from Beurling-Ahlfors transform analysis. The authors achieve sharp estimates (up to factor √3) for general cases and exact sharpness for radial cases.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to determine optimal constants in Korn-type inequalities and explore their relationship with Morrey's problem in calculus of variations. The authors aim to establish dimension-free bounds and sharp estimates for these important inequalities in elasticity theory.
AI.method: The authors adapt techniques from analysis of the Beurling-Ahlfors transform to Korn's inequality. They use a weighted version of Burkholder's differential subordination theorem (recently introduced in 2025) to prove dimension-free weighted versions for Muckenhoupt weights.
AI.result: Main results: 1) Korn's inequality constant admits dimension-free bound; 2) Estimate is sharp up to factor √3; 3) In radial case, estimate is exactly sharp; 4) Several improvements to estimates in various function spaces; 5) Dimension-free weighted version for Muckenhoupt weights.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully establishes dimension-free bounds for Korn-type inequalities with near-sharp estimates, connects these to Morrey's problem, and extends results to weighted settings using modern techniques from harmonic analysis and martingale theory.

title: Statistical Flux Freezing with Magnetic Path-lines in Turbulence
summary: Magnetic flux freezing states that, in ideal magnetohydrodynamics, magnetic flux is transported by the flow and magnetic field lines remain frozen into the plasma. In turbulent plasmas, however, the velocity and magnetic fields are spatially rough, invalidating the regularity assumptions underlying the classical theorem. Previous work has shown that Lagrangian trajectories in such rough flows can become nonunique in the limit of small magnetic diffusivity, leading to stochastic formulations of magnetic flux freezing based on magnetic field lines. Field lines, however, are instantaneous geometric objects that do not possess a natural time evolution and do not preserve identity in turbulent flows. We instead use magnetic path lines, which remain intrinsically stochastic in the ideal limit, implying that magnetic flux is conserved only in a statistical sense over ensembles of backward-advected path-line surfaces. This yields a statistical formulation of Alfven's theorem in terms of magnetic path lines and shows that the classical deterministic form of flux freezing cannot hold in sufficiently rough turbulent magnetic fields. While closely related in physical content to earlier stochastic flux-freezing approaches based on magnetic field lines, the path-line formulation provides time-evolving dynamical trajectories that retain identity in spacetime and offer a simpler and more transparent framework for analyzing stochastic magnetic transport.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that classical magnetic flux freezing fails in turbulent plasmas with rough fields, and proposes a statistical formulation using magnetic path lines instead of field lines.
AI.motivation: Classical magnetic flux freezing assumes smooth velocity and magnetic fields, but turbulent plasmas have spatially rough fields that invalidate these regularity assumptions. Previous stochastic approaches based on magnetic field lines have limitations since field lines are instantaneous geometric objects without natural time evolution or preserved identity in turbulent flows.
AI.method: The authors use magnetic path lines instead of magnetic field lines. Path lines remain intrinsically stochastic in the ideal limit and provide time-evolving dynamical trajectories that retain identity in spacetime. This allows formulation of flux conservation in a statistical sense over ensembles of backward-advected path-line surfaces.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that classical deterministic flux freezing cannot hold in sufficiently rough turbulent magnetic fields. The path-line formulation yields a statistical version of Alfven's theorem, showing magnetic flux is conserved only statistically over ensembles.
AI.conclusion: The magnetic path-line approach provides a simpler, more transparent framework for analyzing stochastic magnetic transport in turbulent plasmas compared to earlier field-line based stochastic formulations, while offering time-evolving trajectories that preserve identity.

title: A Residual-Attention Physics-Informed Neural Network for Irregular Interfaces and Multi-Peak Transport Fields
summary: In complex engineering systems such as electro-thermal-fluid coupling, rapid and accurate prediction of multi-physics fields is essential for advanced applications like digital twins and real-time condition monitoring. Traditional numerical methods often suffer from high computational latency, whereas standard Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) frequently fail to capture critical local features, such as irregular interfaces, localized high-gradient regions, and multi-peak transport structures. To address these limitations and provide high-fidelity intelligent predictions for engineering decision-making, this paper proposes a Residual-Attention Physics-Informed Neural Network (RA-PINN) as a powerful surrogate modeling engine. The proposed method incorporates residual learning and attention enhancement into the network backbone to improve the representation of oblique transition structures, narrow charge layers, and distributed hotspots while strictly preserving global field consistency. To evaluate its effectiveness as an intelligent prediction framework, three representative benchmark cases are constructed, including an oblique asymmetric interface, a bipolar high-gradient charge layer, and a multi-peak Gaussian charge migration field. Under unified training settings, the proposed RA-PINN is systematically compared with a standard pure PINN and an LSTM-PINN in terms of average error, local maximum error, structural similarity, and convergence behavior. The results show that RA-PINN consistently achieves the best overall performance across all benchmark cases, demonstrating its tremendous potential as a highly reliable core inference engine for the condition monitoring and digital twin modeling of complex multi-physics engineering systems.

AI.tldr: RA-PINN (Residual-Attention Physics-Informed Neural Network) improves multi-physics field prediction by combining residual learning and attention mechanisms to capture local features while maintaining global consistency, outperforming standard PINNs and LSTM-PINNs.
AI.motivation: Traditional numerical methods have high computational latency, and standard PINNs fail to capture critical local features like irregular interfaces, high-gradient regions, and multi-peak structures in complex engineering systems like electro-thermal-fluid coupling. There's a need for rapid, accurate predictions for digital twins and real-time condition monitoring.
AI.method: Proposes RA-PINN that incorporates residual learning and attention enhancement into the network backbone to improve representation of oblique transition structures, narrow charge layers, and distributed hotspots while preserving global field consistency. Uses three benchmark cases: oblique asymmetric interface, bipolar high-gradient charge layer, and multi-peak Gaussian charge migration field.
AI.result: RA-PINN consistently achieves best overall performance across all benchmark cases compared to standard PINN and LSTM-PINN in terms of average error, local maximum error, structural similarity, and convergence behavior.
AI.conclusion: RA-PINN demonstrates tremendous potential as a highly reliable core inference engine for condition monitoring and digital twin modeling of complex multi-physics engineering systems.

title: Long-time error analysis of finite element fully discrete schemes for SPDEs with non-globally Lipschitz coefficients
summary: The present paper proposes new fully discrete schemes for long-time approximations of stochastic partial differential equations (SPDEs) with non-globally Lipschitz coefficients in a bounded domain $D \subset \R^d, d =1,2,3 $. A novel family of linearly implicit time-stepping schemes is introduced, based on a standard Galerkin finite element spatial semi-discretization. A distinguishing feature of the schemes is that the proposed finite element fully discrete approximations preserve uniform-in-time moment bounds in a Banach space $L^{r}(D), r >2$, without requiring any restriction on the time-space discretization stepsize ratio. %established... To show it, some non-standard arguments are developed. First, we derive long-time error estimates in the Banach space $L^r(D)$ for finite element fully discrete approximations of the deterministic linear parabolic equation with non-smooth initial value, which is, to our knowledge, new for the literature on numerical PDEs and of independent interest. These error estimates together with the contractive property of the semi-group in $L^{r}(D), r > 2$, the dissipativity of the nonlinearity and the particular benefit of the taming strategy help us establish the desired uniform-in-time moment bounds. Then both strong and weak error bounds of the proposed schemes are carefully analyzed in a setting of low regularity, with uniform-in-time convergence rates obtained for cases of both space-time white and trace-class noises. The analysis is highly nontrivial, due to the finite element discretization, the low regularity and the presence of the super-linearly growing nonlinearity. %in the long-time scenario... the discretization parameters $h$ and $τ$. Finally, numerical results are presented to verify the previous theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: Proposes new linearly implicit time-stepping schemes for SPDEs with non-globally Lipschitz coefficients, preserving uniform-in-time moment bounds without time-space step restrictions.
AI.motivation: Existing numerical methods for SPDEs with non-globally Lipschitz coefficients often face challenges with uniform-in-time moment bounds and require restrictive time-space step ratios. The paper aims to develop schemes that overcome these limitations.
AI.method: Introduces novel linearly implicit time-stepping schemes based on Galerkin finite element spatial discretization. Uses taming strategy for nonlinearity, derives long-time error estimates for deterministic linear parabolic equations, and leverages contractive properties and dissipativity.
AI.result: Schemes preserve uniform-in-time moment bounds in Banach space L^r(D) without step ratio restrictions. Provides both strong and weak error bounds with uniform-in-time convergence rates for space-time white and trace-class noises.
AI.conclusion: The proposed schemes effectively handle SPDEs with non-globally Lipschitz coefficients, achieving uniform-in-time moment bounds and convergence without restrictive discretization conditions. Numerical results validate theoretical findings.

title: Kazdan-Warner obstructions for a 4$th-$order boundary problem
summary: We derive Kazdan-Warner type identities for the boundary problem of prescribing nonconstant interior $Q$ curvature and boundary $T$ curvature on the upper hemisphere $\mathbb{S}^4_+$ by a conformal change of the standard metric. Using the natural variational formulation and conformal variations generated by boundary-preserving conformal vector fields, we obtain nontrivial integral obstructions to solvability.

AI.tldr: Derives Kazdan-Warner type identities for prescribing nonconstant Q-curvature on interior and T-curvature on boundary of upper hemisphere via conformal metric changes, revealing integral obstructions to solvability.
AI.motivation: To understand solvability conditions for prescribing nonconstant Q-curvature on interior and T-curvature on boundary of the upper hemisphere through conformal metric transformations, extending Kazdan-Warner type results to this boundary value problem.
AI.method: Uses variational formulation and conformal variations generated by boundary-preserving conformal vector fields to derive Kazdan-Warner type identities for the boundary value problem on the upper hemisphere.
AI.result: Obtains nontrivial integral obstructions to solvability of the prescribed curvature problem, revealing necessary conditions that must be satisfied for solutions to exist.
AI.conclusion: The derived Kazdan-Warner type identities provide essential integral constraints that must be satisfied for the existence of conformal metrics with prescribed Q-curvature on interior and T-curvature on boundary of the upper hemisphere.

title: Wave-particle equilibria with heavy ions in weakly collisional space plasmas
summary: Space plasmas are weakly collisional since characteristic time scales related to Coulomb collisions are much larger than those of Larmor gyration or wave--particle interactions. Thus, wave activity is likely to drive some of the non-thermal features that are observed in space plasma velocity distributions, such as temperature anisotropy, beams, and skewness. Therefore, we study how wave--particle interactions shape the velocity distribution functions of minor ions, and how these ions and their statistical properties modify the dispersion relation of electromagnetic waves. To achieve this, we derive the motion of heavy ions in electromagnetic waves using the Boris algorithm. We take the waves to be solutions of the fully kinetic dispersion relation of electromagnetic waves in two-ion component plasmas with parameters representative of the solar wind. We use the Arbitrary Linear Plasma Solver (ALPS) code to derive the linear Vlasov--Maxwell dispersion relation based on the actual distribution of the ions. The test-particles are initially in thermal equilibrium, and their distribution evolves due to interactions with the waves. By solving the dispersion relation using the evolved distributions, we show that the system evolves into a steady wave--particle equilibrium, which is characterized by a minimization of the interaction and energy transfer between wave and particles.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how wave-particle interactions shape velocity distributions of minor ions in space plasmas and how these ions modify electromagnetic wave dispersion relations, showing the system evolves to a steady wave-particle equilibrium.
AI.motivation: Space plasmas are weakly collisional, so wave activity likely drives observed non-thermal features like temperature anisotropy, beams, and skewness in velocity distributions. Understanding how wave-particle interactions shape minor ion distributions and how these ions affect wave dispersion is important for space plasma physics.
AI.method: Derived motion of heavy ions in electromagnetic waves using Boris algorithm. Used waves as solutions of fully kinetic dispersion relation in two-ion component plasmas with solar wind parameters. Employed ALPS code to derive linear Vlasov-Maxwell dispersion relation based on actual ion distributions. Test particles started in thermal equilibrium and evolved through wave interactions.
AI.result: The system evolves into a steady wave-particle equilibrium characterized by minimization of interaction and energy transfer between waves and particles. The evolved ion distributions modify the electromagnetic wave dispersion relations.
AI.conclusion: Wave-particle interactions play a crucial role in shaping velocity distributions of minor ions in space plasmas, leading to steady-state equilibria where energy transfer between waves and particles is minimized, providing insights into observed non-thermal features in space plasma distributions.

title: Wafer-to-Wafer Bonding: Part: I -- The Coupled Physics Problem and the 2D Finite Element Implementation
summary: Wafer-to-wafer (WxW) bonding is a key enabler for three-dimensional integration, including hybrid bonding for fine-pitch Cu-Cu interconnects. During bonding, wafer deformation and the air entrapped between the wafers interact through a strongly coupled, time-dependent fluid-structure interaction (FSI) that can produce non-intuitive bonding dynamics and process sensitivities. This paper develops a mathematically consistent reduced-order model for WxW bonding by deriving a Kirchhoff-Love plate equation for wafer bending from three-dimensional linear elasticity and coupling it to a Reynolds lubrication equation for the inter-wafer air film. The resulting nonlinear plate-Reynolds system is discretized and solved monolithically in the high-performance FEniCSx framework using a $C^0$ interior-penalty formulation for the fourth-order plate operator, standard continuous Galerkin discretization for the pressure field, implicit time integration, and a Newton solver with automatic differentiation. Simulations reproduce experimentally reported probe-displacement histories for multiple initial gaps and verify force equilibrium at the bond front, where the Reynolds pressure acts as an effective contact reaction. Parametric studies reveal nonlinear, and in some cases non-monotonic, sensitivities of bonding-front kinetics to the initial gap, air viscosity, and interfacial energy, providing actionable trends for process optimization.

AI.tldr: A reduced-order model for wafer-to-wafer bonding that couples wafer bending (Kirchhoff-Love plate) with inter-wafer air film dynamics (Reynolds lubrication) to predict bonding front kinetics and process sensitivities.
AI.motivation: Wafer-to-wafer bonding is crucial for 3D integration, but the fluid-structure interaction between wafer deformation and entrapped air creates complex, non-intuitive dynamics that need modeling for process optimization.
AI.method: Derived Kirchhoff-Love plate equation from 3D linear elasticity for wafer bending, coupled with Reynolds lubrication equation for air film. Implemented monolithic solution in FEniCSx using C0 interior-penalty for plate, continuous Galerkin for pressure, implicit time integration, and Newton solver with automatic differentiation.
AI.result: Model reproduces experimental probe-displacement histories, verifies force equilibrium at bond front, and reveals nonlinear (sometimes non-monotonic) sensitivities of bonding kinetics to initial gap, air viscosity, and interfacial energy.
AI.conclusion: The developed reduced-order model successfully captures wafer-to-wafer bonding dynamics and provides actionable insights for process optimization by quantifying how key parameters affect bonding front kinetics.

title: Arbitrary order stationarity preserving stabilized finite elements for multidimensional nonlinear hyperbolic problems. Application to the Euler equations with gravity
summary: We develop arbitrarily high-order, stationarity-preserving stabilized finite element methods for multidimensional nonlinear hyperbolic balance laws on Cartesian grids. We aim at approximating all the steady states of the problem at hand, including non-trivial genuinely multidimensional equilibria, with a level of accuracy higher than the nominal one of the underlying scheme. We formalize more precisely the meaning of stationarity preservation, providing some technical conditions for its realizability. We then recast the multidimensional global-flux quadrature of Barsukow et al. (Num. Meth. PDEs, 2025) as a local preprocessing of the physical fluxes that maps continuous polynomial vector fields to a local space with Raviart--Thomas-type structure. Both the Galerkin and SUPG formulations are recast in this setting. The resulting methods extend the stationarity-preserving finite-volume approach of Barsukow et al. (J. Comput. Phys., 2026) to high-order continuous finite elements and Barsukow et al. (Num. Meth. PDEs, 2025) to nonlinear balance laws. We analyze key properties of the proposed schemes, including local conservation and nodal superconvergence of the discrete steady kernel, and we discuss their relation to low-Mach-compliant discretizations. We apply the framework to the compressible Euler equations with gravity. A simple source-term reformulation yields machine-precision preservation of isothermal hydrostatic equilibria. Extensive numerical benchmarks, including moving equilibrium, near-equilibrium, and instability-dominated regimes, demonstrate clear improvements in robustness and accuracy over standard SUPG and reference finite-volume methods.

AI.tldr: High-order stabilized finite element methods for nonlinear hyperbolic balance laws that preserve steady states with accuracy beyond nominal scheme order, using flux preprocessing with Raviart-Thomas structure.
AI.motivation: To develop high-order finite element methods that can accurately approximate all steady states of nonlinear hyperbolic balance laws, including non-trivial multidimensional equilibria, while maintaining stationarity preservation properties.
AI.method: Recast multidimensional global-flux quadrature as local preprocessing of physical fluxes mapping to Raviart-Thomas-type structure; extend stationarity-preserving finite-volume approach to high-order continuous finite elements; apply to compressible Euler equations with gravity using source-term reformulation.
AI.result: Methods achieve machine-precision preservation of isothermal hydrostatic equilibria; demonstrate improved robustness and accuracy over standard SUPG and reference finite-volume methods in moving equilibrium, near-equilibrium, and instability-dominated regimes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully extends stationarity-preserving approaches to high-order finite elements for nonlinear balance laws, providing superior steady-state preservation and accuracy while maintaining key properties like local conservation and nodal superconvergence.

title: Solitary Waves for Nonlocal Derivative Nonlinear Schrödinger Equations: A Variational Characterization
summary: We establish several existence results for traveling-wave solutions of the nonlocal derivative nonlinear Schrödinger equation by means of the Nehari manifold technique and the concentration-compactness lemma. We study associated minimization problems in the subcritical and critical cases and prove the existence of at least one minimizer in each case. In the critical case, we additionally obtain explicit solitary-wave solutions for a family of parameters. Finally, we derive Pohozaev-type identities and use them to establish corresponding nonexistence results.

AI.tldr: Existence and nonexistence results for traveling-wave solutions of nonlocal derivative nonlinear Schrödinger equation using variational methods.
AI.motivation: To establish existence results for traveling-wave solutions in nonlocal derivative nonlinear Schrödinger equations, which are important in mathematical physics and nonlinear wave theory.
AI.method: Uses Nehari manifold technique and concentration-compactness lemma to study minimization problems in subcritical and critical cases.
AI.result: Proves existence of at least one minimizer in both subcritical and critical cases, obtains explicit solitary-wave solutions for parameter family in critical case, and establishes nonexistence results via Pohozaev-type identities.
AI.conclusion: Successfully establishes comprehensive existence and nonexistence theory for traveling-wave solutions using variational methods and Pohozaev identities.

title: CMA-Unfold A Covariance Matrix Adaptation unfolding algorithm for stacked calorimeter detectors
summary: Stacking calorimeters also refered as bremsstrahlung cannons widely used in inertial confinement fusion and ultra-intense laser plasma experiments have become essential diagnostics for characterizing short bursts of high-energy photons and charged particles. Extracting the underlying energy spectrum from these detectors requires solving an ill-posed inverse problem, often complicated by noise, secondary particle contamination, and uncertainties in the detector response. In this work, we introduce an open-source unfolding framework available under the username ggfauvel under CMA-unfold based on the Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy (CMA-ES), designed to reconstruct photon spectra directly from depth-dose profiles without imposing restrictive parametric assumptions. The algorithm demonstrates high robustness, accurately recovering complex spectral shapes while tolerating percent-level deviations in individual detector layers. This approach provides a flexible and noise-resilient tool for the analysis of stacking calorimeter data, with particular relevance for bremsstrahlung diagnostics in inertial confinement fusion and high-intensity laser applications.

AI.tldr: Open-source unfolding framework using CMA-ES to reconstruct photon spectra from stacking calorimeter depth-dose profiles without parametric assumptions, showing robustness to noise and detector uncertainties.
AI.motivation: Stacking calorimeters (bremsstrahlung cannons) are essential for characterizing high-energy photons in fusion and laser-plasma experiments, but extracting energy spectra requires solving ill-posed inverse problems complicated by noise, secondary particle contamination, and detector response uncertainties.
AI.method: Developed an open-source unfolding framework (CMA-unfold) based on Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy (CMA-ES) to reconstruct photon spectra directly from depth-dose profiles without imposing restrictive parametric assumptions.
AI.result: The algorithm demonstrates high robustness, accurately recovering complex spectral shapes while tolerating percent-level deviations in individual detector layers. It provides noise-resilient spectral reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: The CMA-ES-based unfolding framework offers a flexible and noise-resilient tool for analyzing stacking calorimeter data, particularly relevant for bremsstrahlung diagnostics in inertial confinement fusion and high-intensity laser applications.

title: Screened second-order exchange in the uniform electron gas: exact reduction, a single-pole reference model and asymptotic analysis
summary: We derive an exact reduction of the screened second-order exchange (SOSEX) energy in the uniform electron gas to a triple integral for a specific class of single-pole screened interaction. The reduction proceeds by rescaling the frequency variable to factorize the propagator denominators, applying a Fourier decomposition to separate the two particle-hole blocks, and finally performing a change of integration variables that brings the geometric structure into a tractable form. The reduction to a one-variable integral kernel is possible if and only if the screened interaction belongs to a one-pole class characterized by a single momentum-independent frequency scale~$μ$, which we call the reduction-compatible single-pole (RC-SP) model. The RC-SP model does not approximate plasmon dispersions in real materials, but provides an exactly reducible reference model for analyzing dynamically screened exchange, and gives natural basis elements for approximating more general one-pole screening. We analyze the $μ$-dependence of the SOSEX energy asymptotically at both small and large~$μ$ and establish the leading behaviors at the theorem level. Under a power-law mapping from~$μ$ to the density parameter~$r_s$, this asymptotic structure constrains the analytic form of the screened-exchange correction in $r_s$-space, providing a diagrammatically justified basis for beyond-RPA functional construction. Direct numerical integration of the reduced representation confirms the asymptotic behaviors quantitatively.

AI.tldr: Exact reduction of screened second-order exchange (SOSEX) energy in uniform electron gas to triple integral for specific single-pole screened interactions, enabling analytic analysis and functional construction.
AI.motivation: To develop an exactly reducible reference model for analyzing dynamically screened exchange in many-body systems, providing a diagrammatically justified basis for beyond-RPA functional construction in density functional theory.
AI.method: Mathematical reduction of SOSEX energy through: 1) rescaling frequency variable to factorize propagator denominators, 2) Fourier decomposition to separate particle-hole blocks, 3) change of integration variables to simplify geometric structure. The reduction is only possible for reduction-compatible single-pole (RC-SP) models with single momentum-independent frequency scale μ.
AI.result: Successfully derived exact reduction to triple integral, analyzed μ-dependence asymptotically at small and large μ, established leading behaviors at theorem level, and confirmed asymptotic behaviors through direct numerical integration of reduced representation.
AI.conclusion: The RC-SP model provides an exactly reducible reference for analyzing dynamically screened exchange, enabling analytic constraints on screened-exchange corrections in r_s-space and serving as natural basis elements for approximating more general one-pole screening in beyond-RPA functional construction.

title: Simple but not Simpler: A Surface-Sliding Method for Finding the Minimum Distance between Two Ellipsoids
summary: We propose a novel iterative process to establish the minimum separation between two ellipsoids. The method maintains one point on each surface and updates their locations in the theta-phi parametric space. The tension along the connecting segment between the two surface points serves as the guidance for the sliding direction, and the distance between them decreases gradually. The minimum distance is established when the connecting segment becomes perpendicular to the ellipsoid surfaces, at which point the net effect of the segment tension disappears and the surface points no longer move. Demonstration examples are carefully designed, and excellent numerical performance is observed, including accuracy, consistency, stability, and robustness. Furthermore, compared to other existing techniques, this surface-sliding approach has several attractive features, such as clear geometric representation, concise formulation, a simple algorithm, and the potential to be extended straightforwardly to other situations. This method is expected to be useful for future studies in computer graphics, engineering design, material modeling, and scientific simulations.

AI.tldr: A novel iterative method for finding the minimum distance between two ellipsoids using surface-sliding points guided by tension along connecting segments.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient and geometrically intuitive method for computing the minimum separation between ellipsoids, which is important for applications in computer graphics, engineering design, material modeling, and scientific simulations.
AI.method: Maintains one point on each ellipsoid surface and iteratively updates their positions in theta-phi parametric space. Uses the tension along the connecting segment between points as guidance for sliding direction, gradually decreasing distance until the segment becomes perpendicular to both surfaces.
AI.result: The method demonstrates excellent numerical performance including accuracy, consistency, stability, and robustness. It converges to minimum distance when the connecting segment becomes perpendicular to both ellipsoid surfaces, at which point surface points stop moving.
AI.conclusion: The surface-sliding approach offers clear geometric representation, concise formulation, simple algorithm, and potential for straightforward extension to other situations, making it useful for various applications requiring ellipsoid distance calculations.

title: Inverse scattering for the fractional Helmholtz equation with cubic nonlinearity
summary: In this article, we study the inverse scattering problem for the nonlinear fractional Helmholtz equation with cubic nonlinearity in three dimensions, where we recover a compactly supported potential from scattering amplitude.

AI.tldr: Inverse scattering problem for 3D nonlinear fractional Helmholtz equation with cubic nonlinearity, recovering compactly supported potential from scattering amplitude.
AI.motivation: Study inverse scattering problems for nonlinear fractional Helmholtz equations, which combine fractional calculus with nonlinear wave phenomena, important for understanding wave propagation in complex media with memory effects.
AI.method: Analyze inverse scattering problem using scattering amplitude data to recover compactly supported potential in three-dimensional nonlinear fractional Helmholtz equation with cubic nonlinearity.
AI.result: Develop theoretical framework for recovering compactly supported potential from scattering amplitude in nonlinear fractional Helmholtz equation setting.
AI.conclusion: Establishes foundation for solving inverse scattering problems in nonlinear fractional Helmholtz equations, contributing to mathematical analysis of wave propagation in fractional media with nonlinear effects.

title: Anisotropic truncation for turbulent transport in the Hasegawa-Wakatani system
summary: Reduced models based on an anisotropic truncation of the Fourier space, retaining only a few poloidal wave-numbers while keeping the full radial resolution, are developed and applied to the Hasegawa-Wakatani system. The impact of the truncation is studied first by considering the fixed-gradient formulation, and by comparing to direct numerical simulations (DNS). The turbulent particle flux, and the transition from the quasi-two dimensional turbulence to the zonal flow (ZF) dominated state, are used as the main criteria for validation. Then, similar reduced models are developed in a flux-driven formulation and compared to the DNS, focusing on two cases far from the non-linear threshold of the transition from turbulence to zonal dominated states of the fixed gradient formulation. In both fixed gradient and flux driven cases, it is found that at least 4 poloidal modes, distributed around the most unstable mode, are needed to reproduce the DNS results reasonably. In the flux-driven case, about 10 modes are needed to recover the probability distribution function of the particle flux of the DNS. Considering the role played by different poloidal scales in the turbulent cascade, it is observed that in the turbulent state, an inverse energy cascade in radial wave-numbers takes place at large poloidal scales, while a forward enstrophy cascade in radial wave-numbers is observed to occur at smaller poloidal scales. Moreover, when they form, ZFs feed on poloidal scales that are around and slightly smaller than the injection scale, while giving their energy to the larger poloidal scales. In that case, there is an anisotropic inverse energy transfer, akin to inverse cascade, from the energy injection to the large poloidal scales through ZFs, while the forward enstrophy cascade seems to stay isotropic.

AI.tldr: Reduced Fourier-space models with limited poloidal wave-numbers but full radial resolution are developed for Hasegawa-Wakatani system. At least 4 poloidal modes are needed to reproduce DNS results in fixed-gradient cases, and about 10 modes for flux-driven cases.
AI.motivation: To develop computationally efficient reduced models for plasma turbulence by truncating Fourier space in the poloidal direction while maintaining full radial resolution, and to understand how many poloidal modes are needed to accurately capture turbulent dynamics.
AI.method: Developed reduced models based on anisotropic truncation of Fourier space (retaining few poloidal wave-numbers while keeping full radial resolution). Applied to Hasegawa-Wakatani system. Studied impact by comparing to DNS in both fixed-gradient and flux-driven formulations, using turbulent particle flux and turbulence-to-zonal-flow transition as validation criteria.
AI.result: At least 4 poloidal modes (distributed around most unstable mode) needed to reproduce DNS results in fixed-gradient cases. In flux-driven cases, about 10 modes needed to recover probability distribution function of particle flux. Observed anisotropic energy transfer: inverse energy cascade in radial wave-numbers at large poloidal scales, forward enstrophy cascade at smaller poloidal scales. Zonal flows feed on poloidal scales around injection scale and transfer energy to larger poloidal scales.
AI.conclusion: Reduced models with limited poloidal modes can reasonably reproduce DNS results, with minimum requirements varying by case (4 modes for fixed-gradient, 10 for flux-driven). The study reveals anisotropic energy transfer mechanisms in plasma turbulence, with zonal flows playing key role in inverse energy transfer from injection to large poloidal scales.

title: Path Integral Monte Carlo on a Sphere
summary: We solve numerically exactly a simple toy model to quantum general relativity or more properly to path integral on a curved space. We consider the thermal equilibrium of a quantum many body problem on the sphere, the surface of constant positive curvature. We use path integral Monte Carlo to measure the kinetic energy, the internal energy and the static structure of a bosons, fermions and anyons fluid at low temperatures on the sphere. For bosons we also measure the superfluid fraction and compare its behavior at the critical temperature with the universal jump predicted by Nelson and Kosterlitz in flat space in the thermodynamic limit at the superfluid phase transition. For fermions and anyons it is necessary to use the restricted path integral recipe in order to overcome the sign problem. Even if this recipe is exact for the non interacting fluid it reduces to just an approximation for an interacting system. And we make the example of the electron gas at low temperature. Snapshots of the many body path configuration during the evolution of the computer experiment show that the ``speed'' of the single particle path near the poles slows down as a consequence of the ``hairy ball theorem'' of Poincaré. The influence of curvature on the thermodynamic and structural properties of the many body fluid is also studied.

AI.tldr: Numerical study of quantum many-body systems on a sphere using path integral Monte Carlo, examining bosons, fermions, and anyons at low temperatures, including superfluid transitions and curvature effects.
AI.motivation: To understand quantum many-body physics on curved spaces (specifically spheres) as a toy model for quantum general relativity and path integrals on curved geometries, exploring how curvature affects quantum fluids.
AI.method: Path integral Monte Carlo simulations on the sphere for bosons, fermions, and anyons at low temperatures. For fermions and anyons, used restricted path integral method to overcome sign problems. Measured kinetic energy, internal energy, static structure, and for bosons, superfluid fraction.
AI.result: Observed curvature effects on particle dynamics (slowing near poles due to "hairy ball theorem"), measured superfluid transitions in bosons compared to flat-space predictions, and studied thermodynamic/structural properties of quantum fluids under curvature. Demonstrated restricted path integral method for fermion/anyon systems.
AI.conclusion: Curvature significantly influences quantum many-body systems, with observable effects on dynamics and thermodynamics. The study provides insights into quantum fluids on curved spaces and demonstrates computational approaches for such systems.

title: Algorithmic warm starts for Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
summary:
Generating samples from a continuous probability density is a central algorithmic problem across statistics, engineering, and the sciences. For high-dimensional settings, Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) is the default algorithm across mainstream software packages. However, despite the extensive line of work on HMC and its widespread empirical success, it remains unclear how many iterations of HMC are required as a function of the dimension $d$. On one hand, a variety of results show that Metropolized HMC converges in $O(d^{1/4})$ iterations from a warm start close to stationarity. On the other hand, Metropolized HMC is significantly slower without a warm start, e.g., requiring $Ω(d^{1/2})$ iterations even for simple target distributions such as isotropic Gaussians. Finding a warm start is therefore the computational bottleneck for HMC.
  We resolve this issue for the well-studied setting of sampling from a probability distribution satisfying strong log-concavity (or isoperimetry) and third-order derivative bounds. We prove that \emph{non-Metropolized} HMC generates a warm start in $\tilde{O}(d^{1/4})$ iterations, after which we can exploit the warm start using Metropolized HMC. Our final complexity of $\tilde{O}(d^{1/4})$ is the fastest algorithm for high-accuracy sampling under these assumptions, improving over the prior best of $\tilde{O}(d^{1/2})$. This closes the long line of work on the dimensional complexity of MHMC for such settings, and also provides a simple warm-start prescription for practical implementations.

AI.tldr: Non-Metropolized HMC generates warm starts in $\tilde{O}(d^{1/4})$ iterations for strongly log-concave distributions, enabling efficient high-dimensional sampling with overall $\tilde{O}(d^{1/4})$ complexity.
AI.motivation: HMC is widely used for high-dimensional sampling but requires warm starts for optimal performance. Without warm starts, HMC suffers from $\Omega(d^{1/2})$ iteration complexity even for simple distributions like isotropic Gaussians, making warm start generation the computational bottleneck.
AI.method: The paper proposes using non-Metropolized HMC to generate warm starts in $\tilde{O}(d^{1/4})$ iterations for strongly log-concave distributions with third-order derivative bounds. After obtaining the warm start, Metropolized HMC can be applied for efficient sampling.
AI.result: The approach achieves $\tilde{O}(d^{1/4})$ overall complexity for high-accuracy sampling, improving over the prior best of $\tilde{O}(d^{1/2})$. This resolves the dimensional complexity question for HMC under these assumptions and provides a practical warm-start prescription.
AI.conclusion: The paper closes the long-standing question about HMC's dimensional complexity for strongly log-concave distributions, demonstrating that non-Metropolized HMC can efficiently generate warm starts, leading to optimal $\tilde{O}(d^{1/4})$ sampling complexity.

title: The Benjamin-Feir instability in KdV-like equations with general dispersion and monomial nonlinearity
summary: Nonlinear waves in dispersive media can be succeptible to modulational instabilities. We examine a category of scalar equations, with general dispersion and monomial nonlinearity, including a large variety of KdV-like equations. For small-amplitude traveling wave solutions, we provide a complete characterization of the spectrum near the origin of the linear operator obtained from linearizing about periodic traveling waves. We prove rigorously that, when the modulational instability is present, the spectrum connected to the origin consists of curves that invariably form a closed figure-eight pattern.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes modulational instability in dispersive nonlinear wave equations, showing that unstable spectra near the origin always form a closed figure-eight pattern.
AI.motivation: To understand the spectral structure near the origin for linear operators obtained from linearizing about periodic traveling wave solutions in dispersive nonlinear wave equations, particularly focusing on modulational instability patterns.
AI.method: Examine a category of scalar equations with general dispersion and monomial nonlinearity (including KdV-like equations). Analyze small-amplitude traveling wave solutions and characterize the spectrum near the origin of the linear operator from linearization about periodic traveling waves.
AI.result: Complete characterization of the spectrum near the origin. When modulational instability is present, the spectrum connected to the origin consists of curves that invariably form a closed figure-eight pattern.
AI.conclusion: The figure-eight pattern is a universal feature of modulational instability spectra in this class of dispersive nonlinear wave equations, providing rigorous mathematical confirmation of this characteristic structure.

title: Optical modelling of shaped laser pulses in plasma
summary: This work provides a brief review of the numerical methods for modelling the optical propagation of an ultrashort, intense laser pulse in plasmas and ionizable gases. These methods are implemented in an open-source simulation toolkit Axiprop, which is now actively used for design studies in the context of laser plasma acceleration of electrons (LPA). We present two examples of such studies: optical plasma waveguide generation, and phase-locked flying-focus LPA. These tools enable the identification of complex effects impacting both energy deposition during waveguide formation, and the pulse propagation dynamics that governs wakefield structure, ultimately determining the properties of the accelerated electron bunches. The developed tools and presented simulation designs are relevant to experiments on laser plasma interactions at modern high power laser facilities.

AI.tldr: Review of numerical methods for modeling ultrashort intense laser pulse propagation in plasmas/gases, implemented in open-source toolkit Axiprop for laser plasma acceleration studies.
AI.motivation: To provide computational tools for studying laser plasma acceleration (LPA) by developing methods to model optical propagation of intense laser pulses in plasmas and ionizable gases.
AI.method: Developed numerical methods implemented in open-source simulation toolkit Axiprop, with applications demonstrated through two case studies: optical plasma waveguide generation and phase-locked flying-focus LPA.
AI.result: Created tools that enable identification of complex effects impacting energy deposition during waveguide formation and pulse propagation dynamics affecting wakefield structure, which determines accelerated electron bunch properties.
AI.conclusion: The developed Axiprop toolkit and simulation designs are relevant for experiments on laser plasma interactions at modern high-power laser facilities.

title: Radial Gausslets
summary: Gausslets are one of the few examples of basis sets for electronic structure which allow for two-index/diagonal electron-electron interaction terms. A weakness of gausslets is that, because of their 1D origin, they have been tied to Cartesian coordinates. Here we generalize the gausslet construction for the radial coordinate in three dimensions for atomic basis sets. These radial gausslets make a very compact radial basis with a relatively modest number of functions, with diagonal interaction terms. We illustrate the accuracy of this construction with Hartree--Fock and exact diagonalization on atomic systems.

AI.tldr: Generalizes 1D gausslets to radial coordinates for 3D atomic basis sets, creating compact radial bases with diagonal electron-electron interactions.
AI.motivation: Gausslets have been limited to Cartesian coordinates due to their 1D origin, restricting their application in atomic systems where radial coordinates are more natural.
AI.method: Develops radial gausslets by generalizing the gausslet construction specifically for the radial coordinate in three dimensions, maintaining the property of diagonal electron-electron interaction terms.
AI.result: Creates a compact radial basis requiring relatively few functions while preserving diagonal interaction terms, demonstrated through Hartree-Fock and exact diagonalization calculations on atomic systems.
AI.conclusion: Radial gausslets successfully extend gausslet basis sets to atomic systems, providing an efficient radial basis with diagonal electron-electron interactions for accurate quantum chemistry calculations.

title: Generalization Bounds for Physics-Informed Neural Networks for the Incompressible Navier-Stokes Equations
summary: This work establishes rigorous first-of-its-kind upper bounds on the generalization error for the method of approximating solutions to the (d+1)-dimensional incompressible Navier-Stokes equations by training depth-2 neural networks trained via the unsupervised Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) framework. This is achieved by bounding the Rademacher complexity of the PINN risk. For appropriately weight bounded net classes our derived generalization bounds do not explicitly depend on the network width and our framework characterizes the generalization gap in terms of the fluid's kinematic viscosity and loss regularization parameters. In particular, the resulting sample complexity bounds are dimension-independent. Our generalization bounds suggest using novel activation functions for solving fluid dynamics. We provide empirical validation of the suggested activation functions and the corresponding bounds on a PINN setup solving the Taylor-Green vortex benchmark.

AI.tldr: First rigorous generalization bounds for PINNs solving (d+1)-D Navier-Stokes equations with dimension-independent sample complexity
AI.motivation: To establish theoretical foundations for Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) in solving high-dimensional fluid dynamics problems by providing rigorous generalization error bounds
AI.method: Bounding Rademacher complexity of PINN risk for depth-2 neural networks with weight constraints, deriving generalization bounds in terms of kinematic viscosity and regularization parameters
AI.result: Dimension-independent sample complexity bounds that don't explicitly depend on network width, suggesting novel activation functions for fluid dynamics
AI.conclusion: Theoretical framework provides rigorous generalization guarantees for PINNs in fluid dynamics, validated empirically on Taylor-Green vortex benchmark with suggested activation functions

title: A Lavrentiev phenomenon in the neo-Hookean model
summary: We exhibit a Lavrentiev gap phenomenon for the neo-Hookean energy in three-dimensional nonlinear elasticity. More precisely, we construct boundary data for which the infimum of the neo-Hookean energy over deformations satisfying a natural regularity and invertibility condition is strictly larger than the infimum over the weak $H^1$-closure of that class. The mechanism underlying the gap is a deformation with a dipole-type singularity.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates a Lavrentiev gap phenomenon for neo-Hookean elasticity, showing that the infimum energy over regular invertible deformations is strictly larger than over their weak H¹ closure, with a dipole-type singularity causing the gap.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether the Lavrentiev gap phenomenon (where infimum energies differ between different function spaces) occurs in three-dimensional nonlinear elasticity, specifically for the neo-Hookean energy model.
AI.method: Construct specific boundary data and analyze the energy minimization problem. The key mechanism involves constructing a deformation with a dipole-type singularity that belongs to the weak H¹ closure but not to the original class of regular invertible deformations.
AI.result: Successfully exhibited a Lavrentiev gap for neo-Hookean energy in 3D elasticity. The infimum over regular invertible deformations is strictly larger than the infimum over their weak H¹ closure, demonstrating the existence of this mathematical phenomenon.
AI.conclusion: The Lavrentiev gap exists in three-dimensional nonlinear elasticity for neo-Hookean materials, with dipole-type singularities playing a crucial role in creating the energy difference between function spaces.

title: Frequency Chirping of Energetic-Particle-Driven Geodesic Acoustic Modes in Tokamaks
summary: A suprathermal population of ions is present in tokamak plasmas due to external heating mechanisms and fusion reactions. These energetic particles (EP) can drive wave unstable, via inverse Landau damping. An example is the energetic-particledriven geodesic acoustic mode (EGAMs). In this work, we perform a systematic gyrokinetic investigation of the EGAM linear and nonlinear dynamics, using the global gyrokinetic particle-in-cell code ORB5. The nonlinear saturation given by the EP redistribution in phase space is characterized by a saturation level scaling quadratically with respect to the linear growth rate. The nonlinear EP dynamics in phase space has also effects on the EGAM frequency. To this extent, we investigate the frequency chirping, and we find that the chirping rate scales linearly with the linear growth rate over a wide range of EP concentrations. This scaling is consistent with the theoretical prediction of Chen-Zonca [L. Chen, and F. Zonca, Rev. Mod. Phys. 88, 015008 (2016)].

AI.tldr: EGAM nonlinear dynamics study using gyrokinetic simulations shows quadratic scaling of saturation level with growth rate and linear scaling of frequency chirping rate with growth rate.
AI.motivation: Energetic particles in tokamak plasmas can drive instabilities like EGAMs, which need to be understood for plasma control and confinement optimization.
AI.method: Systematic gyrokinetic investigation using global gyrokinetic particle-in-cell code ORB5 to study EGAM linear and nonlinear dynamics, focusing on EP redistribution in phase space.
AI.result: Nonlinear saturation level scales quadratically with linear growth rate; frequency chirping rate scales linearly with linear growth rate across various EP concentrations.
AI.conclusion: The scaling relationships (quadratic for saturation, linear for chirping) align with theoretical predictions, providing validation for understanding EGAM dynamics in tokamak plasmas.

title: Profound impacts of interlayer interactions in bilayer altermagnetic V2S2O
summary: Two-dimensional altermagnets exhibit exceptional potential for low-power spintronics via nonrelativistic spin splitting and zero net magnetization. Here, we systematically investigate the influence of interlayer interactions on the electronic, magnetic and quantum transport properties of bilayer vanadium oxysulfide (V2S2O), a prototypical layered altermagnet, using DFT and NEGF calculations. Our results reveal that interlayer interactions predominantly modulate the p-orbital derived top valence bands, inducing a profound competitive valence band maximum position between Gamma-point pz and X/Y-point pxy orbitals, with an energy difference as small as 9 meV. Furthermore, interlayer interactions suppress the piezomagnetic effect and impose additional requirements on the type of strain for the bilayer system, compared to its monolayer counterpart. Out-of-plane external electric fields effectively weaken interlayer coupling by enlarging the energy difference of Gamma/X-Y top valence bands to 170 meV. Quantum transport simulations on a bilayer Au/V2S2O/Au two-probe device demonstrate the presence of pronounced spin current. Interlayer interactions reduce the transmission spin polarization from nearly 100% (monolayer) to 60% (bilayer) for energies above the Fermi level. Notably, gate-voltage modulation exhibits significant asymmetry in controlling charge-to-spin current conversion efficiency, originating from the out-of-plane symmetry breaking induced by the electrode geometry. Specifically, a positive gate voltage markedly enhances the contribution of the bottom layer to the overall spin polarization, while a negative gate voltage induces a marginal reduction of transmission spin polarization, attributed to the inherently weak polarization contribution of the bottom layer. These findings provide essential insights for the design and optimization of multilayer altermagnetic spintronics.

AI.tldr: Bilayer V2S2O altermagnet shows interlayer interactions modulate valence bands, suppress piezomagnetism, and reduce spin polarization from 100% to 60%, with asymmetric gate-voltage control of spin current conversion.
AI.motivation: To understand how interlayer interactions affect electronic, magnetic, and quantum transport properties in bilayer altermagnets, which have potential for low-power spintronics applications.
AI.method: Used DFT and NEGF calculations to systematically investigate bilayer V2S2O, including quantum transport simulations on Au/V2S2O/Au two-probe devices with gate-voltage modulation.
AI.result: Interlayer interactions modulate p-orbital valence bands (9 meV energy difference), suppress piezomagnetic effects, reduce spin polarization from 100% to 60%, and show asymmetric gate-voltage control where positive voltage enhances bottom layer contribution.
AI.conclusion: Interlayer interactions significantly impact altermagnetic properties, providing essential insights for designing and optimizing multilayer altermagnetic spintronic devices.

title: High-Resolution Tensor-Network Fourier Methods for Exponentially Compressed Non-Gaussian Aggregate Distributions
summary: Characteristic functions of weighted sums of independent random variables exhibit low-rank structure in the quantized tensor train (QTT) representation, also known as matrix product states (MPS), enabling up to exponential compression of their fully non-Gaussian probability distributions. Under variable independence, the global characteristic function factorizes into local terms. Its low-rank QTT structure arises from intrinsic spectral smoothness in continuous models, or from spectral energy concentration as the number of components $D$ grows in discrete models. We demonstrate this on weighted sums of Bernoulli and lognormal random variables. In the former, despite an adversarial, incompressible small-$D$ regime, the characteristic function undergoes a sharp bond-dimension collapse for $D \gtrsim 300$ components, enabling polylogarithmic time and memory scaling. In the latter, the approach reaches high-resolution discretizations of $N = 2^{30}$ frequency modes on standard hardware, far beyond the $N = 2^{24}$ ceiling of dense implementations. These compressed representations enable efficient computation of Value at Risk (VaR) and Expected Shortfall (ES), supporting applications in quantitative finance and beyond.

AI.tldr: Characteristic functions of weighted sums of independent random variables have low-rank QTT/MPS structure, enabling exponential compression for efficient computation of financial risk measures.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to overcome computational bottlenecks in evaluating probability distributions of weighted sums of independent random variables, which is crucial for applications like quantitative finance where computing Value at Risk (VaR) and Expected Shortfall (ES) requires handling high-dimensional distributions.
AI.method: The method uses quantized tensor train (QTT) / matrix product states (MPS) representations to exploit low-rank structure in characteristic functions. For independent variables, the global characteristic function factorizes into local terms, and its QTT structure emerges from spectral smoothness in continuous models or spectral energy concentration in discrete models as the number of components grows.
AI.result: The approach demonstrates: 1) For weighted sums of Bernoulli variables, a sharp bond-dimension collapse occurs for D ≳ 300 components, enabling polylogarithmic time and memory scaling despite an adversarial small-D regime. 2) For lognormal variables, the method reaches discretizations of N = 2³⁰ frequency modes on standard hardware, far beyond the N = 2²⁴ ceiling of dense implementations.
AI.conclusion: Compressed QTT/MPS representations of characteristic functions enable efficient computation of financial risk measures (VaR and ES), supporting applications in quantitative finance and beyond by overcoming previous computational limitations through exponential compression.

title: Transition fronts of monotone bistable reaction-diffusion systems around an obstacle
summary: This paper is concerned with the interaction between a planar traveling front and a compact obstacle for monotone bistable reaction-diffusion systems in exterior domains. By constructing appropriate sub- and supersolutions, we first establish the existence, uniqueness and monotonicity of the entire solution emanating from a planar traveling front. In particular, we verify that regardless of the shape of the obstacle, the entire solution locally converges to a stationary solution as time tends to infinity. Under the complete propagation assumption, we further show that the entire solution recovers to the same planar traveling front as time tends to infinity after passing the obstacle, and it constitutes a transition front. In addition, we provide some geometric conditions on the obstacle to ensure that the complete propagation assumption is nonempty. Finally, we apply our theoretical results to the Lotka-Volterra competition-diffusion system.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how planar traveling fronts interact with compact obstacles in bistable reaction-diffusion systems, showing the front eventually recovers after passing obstacles under certain conditions.
AI.motivation: To understand how traveling wave fronts in bistable reaction-diffusion systems behave when encountering obstacles in exterior domains, particularly whether they recover their original shape after passing obstacles.
AI.method: Construct appropriate sub- and supersolutions to establish existence, uniqueness and monotonicity of entire solutions; analyze convergence properties; provide geometric conditions on obstacles; apply to Lotka-Volterra competition-diffusion system.
AI.result: 1) Entire solutions converge locally to stationary solutions regardless of obstacle shape; 2) Under complete propagation assumption, solutions recover to same planar traveling front after passing obstacle; 3) Solutions constitute transition fronts; 4) Geometric conditions ensure complete propagation assumption is nonempty.
AI.conclusion: Planar traveling fronts in bistable systems can recover their original shape after passing compact obstacles under appropriate conditions, with applications to biological systems like Lotka-Volterra competition models.

title: Ultrafast electrically controlled magnetism in charge-order-induced ferroelectric altermagnet
summary: The altermagnetism with antiparallel spin alignment exhibits anisotropic spin splitting and may possess an insulating state with a high Neel temperature, while the charge-order-induced ferroelectricity has ultrafast electric polarization switching. Considering that altermagnetism requires breaking space inversion,, the physical foundation for exploring ultrafast electrically controlled magnetism in altermagnetic ferroelectric materials is thus established. In this Letter, based on symmetry analysis and first-principles electronic structure calculations, we predict that LiV$_2$F$_6$ is a material that simultaneously hosts altermagnetism and charge-order-induced ferroelectricity. Since both the altermagnetism and ferroelectricity originate from charge order, LiV$_2$F$_6$ should exhibit strong magnetoelectric coupling. Our calculations indeed demonstrate that electric polarization reversal can induce band spin-polarization switching in LiV$_2$F$_6$. Moreover, time-dependent density functional theory calculations show that the electric polarization reversal in LiV$_2$F$_6$ occurs in 15 femtoseconds. Consequently, ultrafast electrically controlled magnetism can be realized in LiV$_2$F$_6$. Given that LiV$_2$F$_6$ has already been experimentally synthesized, our work provides a promising material platform for achieving ultrafast electrically controlled magnetism, which might have significant implications for the design of future electronic devices.

AI.tldr: LiV₂F₆ is predicted as a material hosting both altermagnetism and charge-order-induced ferroelectricity, enabling ultrafast electrically controlled magnetism with 15 fs polarization switching.
AI.motivation: To find materials that combine altermagnetism (with anisotropic spin splitting and potential high Néel temperature) and ferroelectricity for ultrafast electrically controlled magnetism, which requires breaking space inversion symmetry.
AI.method: Symmetry analysis and first-principles electronic structure calculations, including time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) for polarization dynamics.
AI.result: LiV₂F₆ is predicted to simultaneously host altermagnetism and charge-order-induced ferroelectricity with strong magnetoelectric coupling. Electric polarization reversal induces band spin-polarization switching and occurs in 15 femtoseconds.
AI.conclusion: LiV₂F₆ provides a promising experimental platform for ultrafast electrically controlled magnetism, with significant implications for future electronic device design.

title: Kinetic Langevin Splitting Schemes for Constrained Sampling
summary: Constrained sampling is an important and challenging task in computational statistics, concerned with generating samples from a distribution under certain constraints. There are numerous types of algorithm aimed at this task, ranging from general Markov chain Monte Carlo, to unadjusted Langevin methods. In this article we propose a series of new sampling algorithms based on the latter of these, specifically the kinetic Langevin dynamics. Our series of algorithms are motivated on advanced numerical methods which are splitting order schemes, which include the BU and BAO families of splitting schemes.Their advantage lies in the fact that they have favorable strong order (bias) rates and computationally efficiency. In particular we provide a number of theoretical insights which include a Wasserstein contraction and convergence results. We are able to demonstrate favorable results, such as improved complexity bounds over existing non-splitting methodologies. Our results are verified through numerical experiments on a range of models with constraints, which include a toy example and Bayesian linear regression.

AI.tldr: Proposes new constrained sampling algorithms based on kinetic Langevin dynamics using splitting order schemes (BU/BAO families) with improved complexity bounds and theoretical guarantees.
AI.motivation: Constrained sampling is challenging but important in computational statistics, with existing methods ranging from MCMC to unadjusted Langevin methods. There's a need for more efficient algorithms with better theoretical properties for sampling under constraints.
AI.method: Develops new sampling algorithms based on kinetic Langevin dynamics using advanced splitting order schemes (BU and BAO families). These splitting schemes offer favorable strong order (bias) rates and computational efficiency.
AI.result: Provides theoretical insights including Wasserstein contraction and convergence results. Demonstrates improved complexity bounds over existing non-splitting methodologies. Validates through numerical experiments on constrained models including a toy example and Bayesian linear regression.
AI.conclusion: The proposed splitting-based kinetic Langevin algorithms offer efficient constrained sampling with strong theoretical guarantees and improved performance over existing methods.

title: V-shaped transition fronts of monotone bistable reaction-diffusion systems in exterior domains
summary: This paper investigates the propagation phenomena of a monotone bistable reaction-diffusion system in an exterior domain of R2. By constructing suitable sub- and supersolutions, we establish the existence and monotonicity of an entire solution originating from a V-shaped traveling front.
