It is further shown that, under the complete propagation condition, this entire solution eventually recovers its V-shaped profile as time tends to infty after passing the obstacle. In particular, we show that the entire solution is a V-shaped transition front whose global mean speed coincides with the planar wave speed.

AI.tldr: Existence and properties of V-shaped traveling fronts in bistable reaction-diffusion systems in 2D exterior domains with obstacles.
AI.motivation: To understand how traveling wave solutions behave when encountering obstacles in two-dimensional domains, specifically investigating whether V-shaped fronts can recover their original profile after passing obstacles.
AI.method: Construct suitable sub- and supersolutions to establish existence and monotonicity of entire solutions originating from V-shaped traveling fronts in exterior domains of R².
AI.result: Proved existence of entire solution from V-shaped front; showed it recovers V-shaped profile after passing obstacle under complete propagation condition; demonstrated it's a V-shaped transition front with global mean speed equal to planar wave speed.
AI.conclusion: V-shaped traveling fronts in bistable reaction-diffusion systems can persist and recover their profile after encountering obstacles, maintaining their characteristic speed and shape properties.

title: Fine-tuning of universal machine-learning interatomic potentials for 2D high-entropy alloys
summary: High-entropy alloys (HEAs) and their two-dimensional counterparts (2D-HEAs) have recently attracted attention due to their tunable properties and catalytic potential, yet their chemical complexity makes direct density functional theory (DFT) calculations computationally prohibitive. The complexity also makes training of machine-learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) challenging, but this could possibly be overcome by employing universal MLIPs as starting point. In this work, we investigate the applicability of universal MLIP models for 2D transition metal sulfide HEAs and develop effective fine-tuning strategies. Training structures are systematically generated and selected, and the performance of universal and fine-tuned models are benchmarked against DFT. We find that all universal MLIPs employed in this work yield unsatisfactory mixing energies without fine-tuning. Applied to the experimentally synthesized (Mo,Ta,Nb,W,V)S$_2$ system, fine-tuned models based on enumerated structures can achieve near-DFT accuracy in predicting mixing energies while enabling Monte-Carlo simulations and random structure sampling at scales inaccessible to DFT.

AI.tldr: Universal MLIPs fail for 2D-HEAs without fine-tuning, but fine-tuned models achieve near-DFT accuracy for (Mo,Ta,Nb,W,V)S2 system, enabling large-scale simulations.
AI.motivation: High-entropy alloys (HEAs) and 2D-HEAs have promising catalytic properties, but their chemical complexity makes direct DFT calculations computationally prohibitive. Training machine-learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) for these complex systems is challenging, but universal MLIPs could serve as a starting point.
AI.method: Systematically generate and select training structures for 2D transition metal sulfide HEAs. Benchmark universal MLIP models against DFT, then develop effective fine-tuning strategies. Apply to experimentally synthesized (Mo,Ta,Nb,W,V)S2 system.
AI.result: All universal MLIPs yield unsatisfactory mixing energies without fine-tuning. Fine-tuned models based on enumerated structures achieve near-DFT accuracy in predicting mixing energies. These models enable Monte-Carlo simulations and random structure sampling at scales inaccessible to DFT.
AI.conclusion: Universal MLIPs alone are insufficient for 2D-HEAs, but fine-tuning strategies can transform them into accurate tools for large-scale simulations of complex 2D high-entropy alloy systems.

title: Exact analytical PGSE signal for diffusion confined to a cylindrical surface using a spectral Laplacian formalism
summary: Pulsed-gradient spin-echo (PGSE) MRI experiments probe molecular self-diffusion through spin phase accumulation under time-dependent magnetic field gradients. For diffusion confined to cylindrical surfaces, existing analytical signal models typically rely on the narrow-pulse limit, approximate treatments of finite gradient durations, or the Gaussian phase approximation, which become increasingly inaccurate at high diffusion weightings. Here, we derive an exact analytical solution of the Bloch-Torrey equation for diffusion confined to a cylindrical surface under finite PGSE gradients and obtain the corresponding diffusion MRI signal expression valid for arbitrary gradient durations and separations. The derivation is based on a spectral matrix formalism of the Laplace operator in the eigenbasis of the confining geometry. The signal is expressed as a product of non-commuting matrix exponentials, without approximations to the diffusion propagator or the spin phase distribution. We further introduce a reduced real spectral basis exploiting the symmetry of the cylindrical surface, substantially improving computational efficiency. Building on this exact formulation, we develop efficient numerical strategies for repeated signal evaluations, including a Strang splitting approximation of the matrix exponentials and an efficient computation of the spherical mean signal using Gauss-Legendre quadrature. The analytical signal is validated against Monte Carlo simulations over a wide range of cylinder radii and experimental parameters. The accelerated implementations are benchmarked against the exact formulation to quantify accuracy-runtime trade-offs. These results establish a computationally efficient framework for evaluating directional and orientationally averaged diffusion MRI signals in applications requiring large numbers of model evaluations.

AI.tldr: Exact analytical solution for diffusion MRI signal from molecules confined to cylindrical surfaces under finite gradient pulses, with efficient computational implementation.
AI.motivation: Existing analytical models for diffusion MRI signals from cylindrical surfaces rely on approximations (narrow-pulse limit, Gaussian phase approximation) that become inaccurate at high diffusion weightings, creating a need for exact solutions.
AI.method: Derived exact analytical solution of Bloch-Torrey equation using spectral matrix formalism of Laplace operator in eigenbasis of cylindrical geometry. Developed computational strategies including Strang splitting approximation and Gauss-Legendre quadrature for spherical mean signal.
AI.result: Validated exact solution against Monte Carlo simulations across wide parameter ranges. Benchmarked accelerated implementations showing accuracy-runtime trade-offs, establishing computationally efficient framework.
AI.conclusion: Provides exact analytical framework for diffusion MRI signals from cylindrical surfaces with efficient computational methods suitable for applications requiring many model evaluations.

title: Transition fronts of combustion reaction-diffusion equations in domains with multiple cylindrical branches
summary: This paper is concerned with the propagation phenomenon of the combustion reaction-diffusion equations in domains with multiple cylindrical branches. We first show that there is an entire solution emanating from planar traveling fronts in some branches. Then we prove that the entire solution is a transition front and converges to some planar traveling fronts (with some finite shifts) in the rest branches as time goes to $+\infty$ if the propagation is complete.In addition, by providing the complete propagation of every front-like solution coming from one branch, it is proved that any transition front connecting $0$ and $1$ in domains with multiple cylindrical branches propagates completely and has a unique global mean speed which turns out to be equal to the planar wave speed. Finally, we give some sufficient conditions to ensure that the assumptions on complete propagation are not empty.

AI.tldr: The paper studies combustion reaction-diffusion equations in domains with multiple cylindrical branches, analyzing propagation phenomena, entire solutions, transition fronts, and global mean speeds.
AI.motivation: To understand how combustion fronts propagate in complex domains with multiple cylindrical branches, which is important for applications in combustion theory and pattern formation in complex geometries.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis of reaction-diffusion equations in domains with multiple cylindrical branches, proving existence of entire solutions emanating from planar traveling fronts, analyzing transition fronts, and establishing conditions for complete propagation.
AI.result: Shows existence of entire solutions from planar fronts, proves transition fronts converge to planar fronts with finite shifts, demonstrates any transition front propagates completely with unique global mean speed equal to planar wave speed, and provides sufficient conditions for complete propagation.
AI.conclusion: In domains with multiple cylindrical branches, combustion fronts propagate completely with the same speed as planar waves, and transition fronts converge to shifted planar fronts, with mathematical conditions ensuring these propagation phenomena occur.

title: A numerical study on the coefficient of restitution of wet collisions
summary: Using smoothed particle hydrodynamics (SPH) simulations, we investigate the coefficient of restitution (COR) in wet collisions and identify a scaling law governing its behavior. The simulations employ an updated-Lagrangian, mesh-free framework that is validated against experimental measurements. We neglect surface tension effects since the impact conditions correspond to a moderate-to-high Weber number regime. The COR is found to depend on the Stokes number and a dimensionless film thickness defined as the ratio of the liquid film thickness to the diameter of the impacting solid bead. Two distinct regimes are observed, each characterized by different power-law exponents.

AI.tldr: SPH simulations reveal scaling law for coefficient of restitution in wet collisions, showing dependence on Stokes number and dimensionless film thickness with two distinct power-law regimes.
AI.motivation: To understand the physics of wet collisions and establish a scaling law for the coefficient of restitution (COR) in such impacts, which is important for various industrial and natural processes involving particle collisions with liquid films.
AI.method: Smoothed particle hydrodynamics (SPH) simulations using an updated-Lagrangian, mesh-free framework validated against experimental measurements. Surface tension effects are neglected due to moderate-to-high Weber number impact conditions.
AI.result: COR depends on both Stokes number and dimensionless film thickness (liquid film thickness to bead diameter ratio). Two distinct regimes are observed, each characterized by different power-law exponents in the scaling relationship.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully identifies a scaling law for wet collision restitution coefficients and reveals two distinct physical regimes, providing fundamental understanding of energy dissipation mechanisms in liquid-mediated particle impacts.

title: Nonlinear Stability of Taylor-Couette Flows with Heat Buoyancy
summary: This paper investigates the nonlinear stability of Taylor-Couette (TC) flows incorporating the thermal buoyancy within an annular domain characterized by small viscosity $ν$ and thermal diffusivity $μ$. It is well established that the buoyancy induced convection significantly impacts practical industrial applications of Taylor-Couette flow \cite{Chen2006}. In contrast to \cite{An.2024}, we specifically examines the influence of the temperature gradients and the gravity on the stability of Taylor-Couette flows in this article. The thermal buoyancy term introduces a destabilizing radial derivative $\partial_r$ into the rotating TC system. To mitigate this destabilizing effect, we employ estimates involving the negative derivatives. Consequently, the additional viscous damping becomes necessary to counterbalance the buoyancy induced instability. Our stability criterion requires that the initial perturbations from the Taylor-Couette flow are bounded by a suitable power of the viscosity. Under this condition, we prove that solutions to the 2D Boussinesq system on $[1, R] \times \mathbb{S}^1$ remain close to the Taylor-Couette flow at the same order.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the nonlinear stability of Taylor-Couette flows with thermal buoyancy effects in low viscosity/diffusivity regimes, showing that additional viscous damping is needed to counteract buoyancy-induced instability.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand how temperature gradients and gravity affect the stability of Taylor-Couette flows, which is important for industrial applications where buoyancy-induced convection significantly impacts flow behavior.
AI.method: The authors examine the 2D Boussinesq system in an annular domain [1, R] × 𝕊¹ with small viscosity ν and thermal diffusivity μ. They use estimates involving negative derivatives to mitigate the destabilizing radial derivative introduced by thermal buoyancy, requiring additional viscous damping to counterbalance buoyancy-induced instability.
AI.result: Under the condition that initial perturbations from Taylor-Couette flow are bounded by a suitable power of viscosity, the authors prove that solutions to the 2D Boussinesq system remain close to the Taylor-Couette flow at the same order.
AI.conclusion: Thermal buoyancy introduces destabilizing effects in Taylor-Couette flows that require additional viscous damping to maintain stability, and the stability criterion depends on initial perturbations being sufficiently small relative to viscosity.

title: Relativistic Virial Operators
summary: When studying Dirac operators, it is well known that the phenomenon of Zitterbewegung leads to a lack of convexity of the variance, which creates difficulties in the analysis of dispersive properties. In particular, standard virial methods are harder to implement in the Dirac setting. In this paper, we introduce a new approach based on the center-of-energy operator, leading to a family of relativistic virial identities. As an application, we establish spectral stability results for perturbed Dirac operators and prove local smoothing estimates for the associated evolution equation.

AI.tldr: New virial method using center-of-energy operator for Dirac operators, overcoming Zitterbewegung convexity issues, with applications to spectral stability and local smoothing estimates.
AI.motivation: Zitterbewegung in Dirac operators causes non-convex variance, making standard virial methods difficult to apply for analyzing dispersive properties.
AI.method: Introduces new approach based on center-of-energy operator, leading to family of relativistic virial identities.
AI.result: Establishes spectral stability results for perturbed Dirac operators and proves local smoothing estimates for associated evolution equation.
AI.conclusion: Center-of-energy operator provides effective virial method for Dirac operators, enabling analysis of dispersive properties despite Zitterbewegung challenges.

title: Reaching for the performance limit of hybrid density functional theory for molecular chemistry
summary: Density functional theory (DFT) offers an exceptional balance between accuracy and efficiency, but practical density functional approximations face an unavoidable trade-off among simplicity, accuracy, and transferability. A systematic protocol is therefore needed to develop functionals that are reliably most accurate within a chosen application domain. Here we present such a protocol by combining constraint enforcement, flexible functional forms, and modern optimization. Applying this strategy to the range-separated hybrid (RSH) meta-GGA framework, we obtain the carefully optimized and appropriately constrained hybrid (COACH) functional. Across broad molecular benchmarks, COACH improves both accuracy and transferability relative to leading RSH meta-GGAs, including \omegaB97M-V, while retaining the computational practicality of its rung. Finally, our analysis of the remaining trade-offs and saturation behavior suggests that further systematic progress will likely require the incorporation of genuinely nonlocal information.

AI.tldr: COACH functional improves accuracy and transferability over existing range-separated hybrid meta-GGAs while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: DFT approximations face unavoidable trade-offs among simplicity, accuracy, and transferability, requiring systematic protocols to develop reliable functionals for specific application domains.
AI.method: Combined constraint enforcement, flexible functional forms, and modern optimization within the range-separated hybrid meta-GGA framework to create COACH functional.
AI.result: COACH improves both accuracy and transferability across broad molecular benchmarks compared to leading RSH meta-GGAs like ωB97M-V, while retaining computational practicality.
AI.conclusion: Further systematic progress in DFT functionals will likely require incorporation of genuinely nonlocal information, as trade-offs and saturation behavior suggest limitations of current approaches.

title: Blow-up phemomenon for the 3-component Degasperis-Procesi equation
summary: In this paper, we consider the Cauchy problem of the 3-component Degasperis-Procesi equation. Firstly, we discuss a local well-posedness result and a blow-up criterion in the low besov space. Secondly, we study the blow-up phenomenon by using the method which does not require any conservation law. Finally, we investigate some persistence properties.

AI.tldr: Analysis of Cauchy problem for 3-component Degasperis-Procesi equation: local well-posedness, blow-up criteria, blow-up phenomena without conservation laws, and persistence properties.
AI.motivation: To study the mathematical properties of the 3-component Degasperis-Procesi equation, which is an important integrable system in mathematical physics, particularly focusing on its well-posedness, blow-up behavior, and persistence characteristics.
AI.method: 1. Analysis of local well-posedness and blow-up criterion in low Besov spaces. 2. Investigation of blow-up phenomena using methods that don't require conservation laws. 3. Examination of persistence properties of solutions.
AI.result: The paper establishes local well-posedness results, provides blow-up criteria in low Besov spaces, demonstrates blow-up phenomena without relying on conservation laws, and characterizes persistence properties of solutions.
AI.conclusion: The study provides comprehensive mathematical analysis of the 3-component Degasperis-Procesi equation, establishing fundamental properties including well-posedness, blow-up behavior, and persistence characteristics, contributing to the understanding of this important integrable system.

title: Radial Symmetry of Solutions to Quasilinear Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman Equations in $\mathbb{R}^N$
summary: We investigate the quasilinear elliptic Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman equation in $\mathbb{R}^{N}$ \begin{equation*} -\frac{1}{2}Δu+\frac{1}{p}\,|\nabla u|^{p}+u=f(x),\qquad p>1, \end{equation*}% within the optimal growth and uniqueness framework introduced by Bensoussan et al. (1984) for the case $N=1$ and Alvarez (1996) as well as Bensoussan and Frehse (1992) for the case $N\geq 1$. A central result of the paper shows that when $f$ is radially symmetric, the unique admissible solution is itself radially symmetric. The proof relies on the invariance of the operator under rotations, the uniqueness principle, and an averaging argument over the orthogonal group. In addition, we provide explicit barrier constructions, monotone approximation schemes, and local regularity estimates that guarantee the existence of classical solutions. As an illustration, we derive an exact quadratic solution in the case $% f(x)=|x|^{2} $ and $p=2$. Finally, we connect the PDE to its stochastic control interpretation and establish the extension of Alvarez's uniqueness theory to the regime-switching system of Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman equations, proving the global well-posedness in the multi-regime setting.

AI.tldr: The paper studies quasilinear elliptic Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equations in ℝᴺ, proving radial symmetry of solutions for radially symmetric forcing terms, establishing existence via barrier methods and approximation schemes, and extending uniqueness theory to regime-switching systems.
AI.motivation: Extend the optimal growth and uniqueness framework for Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equations from classical results (Bensoussan et al. 1984, Alvarez 1996, Bensoussan and Frehse 1992) to quasilinear elliptic cases with p>1, particularly focusing on symmetry properties and multi-regime extensions.
AI.method: Uses invariance under rotations, uniqueness principle, and averaging over orthogonal group to prove radial symmetry. Employs explicit barrier constructions, monotone approximation schemes, and local regularity estimates for existence. Connects PDE to stochastic control interpretation and extends to regime-switching systems.
AI.result: When f is radially symmetric, the unique admissible solution is radially symmetric. Provides existence of classical solutions via constructive methods. Derives exact quadratic solution for f(x)=|x|² and p=2. Extends Alvarez's uniqueness theory to regime-switching HJB systems, proving global well-posedness in multi-regime setting.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes symmetry properties for quasilinear elliptic HJB equations, provides constructive existence methods, and successfully extends uniqueness theory to more complex regime-switching systems, bridging PDE analysis with stochastic control theory.

title: The inhomogeneous Total Variation Flow with $L^1$-data
summary:
This paper is devoted to the study of the Dirichlet problem for the parabolic equation driven by the $1$--Laplacian operator under minimal integrability assumptions. Specifically, we consider \begin{equation*} u'-\Div(Du/|D u|)=f\qquad\text{ in } (0,+\infty)\timesΩ\,, \end{equation*} where $Ω\subset\R^N$ is a bounded open set with Lipschitz boundary, $u_0\in L^1(Ω)$ is the initial datum, and $f\in L_{loc}^1(0,+\infty; L^1(Ω))$ is the source term.
  We establish the existence and uniqueness of entropy solutions in this low-regularity setting. Our approach relies on an approximation scheme and an entropy formulation adapted to the \mbox{$1$--Laplacian} structure. Additional results include comparison between solutions, further regularity when data have higher integrability and an analysis of the long-time decay of solutions in the homogeneous case.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the Dirichlet problem for the parabolic 1-Laplacian equation with minimal integrability assumptions, establishing existence and uniqueness of entropy solutions.
AI.motivation: To analyze the parabolic equation driven by the 1-Laplacian operator under low regularity conditions (L¹ data), which is challenging due to the singular nature of the 1-Laplacian operator and weak integrability assumptions.
AI.method: Uses an approximation scheme and develops an entropy formulation specifically adapted to the 1-Laplacian structure to handle the singular operator and low regularity data.
AI.result: Proves existence and uniqueness of entropy solutions in the L¹ setting, establishes comparison principles between solutions, shows improved regularity for higher integrability data, and analyzes long-time decay in homogeneous cases.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully develops a robust theory for the parabolic 1-Laplacian equation under minimal integrability assumptions, providing comprehensive results including existence, uniqueness, comparison, regularity, and asymptotic behavior.

title: Existence and nonexistence of infinitely many solutions to elliptic problems with oscillating nonlinearities
summary: We study sharp conditions for the existence and nonexistence of infinitely many nonnegative solutions to the problem $-Δ_p u = λf(u)$ in a bounded domain with Dirichlet boundary conditions, where $f$ is a continuous function with a sequence of positive zeros converging to zero or diverging to infinity. Under a growth condition on the primitive $F(s) = \int_0^s f(t)dt$, we establish ranges of the parameter $λ$ for which infinitely many small or large solutions exist, as well as ranges where no bifurcation from zero or infinity can occur. The existence result is obtained via variational methods for a general class of divergence form operators, while the nonexistence result is established both for the $p$-Laplacian and for uniformly elliptic operators in non-divergence form via an ODE argument.

AI.tldr: The paper studies existence/nonexistence of infinitely many nonnegative solutions to p-Laplacian equations with parameter λ and nonlinearity f having zeros, establishing ranges for small/large solutions via variational methods and ODE arguments.
AI.motivation: To understand sharp conditions for existence and nonexistence of infinitely many nonnegative solutions to nonlinear elliptic problems with p-Laplacian operators, particularly when the nonlinearity f has sequences of positive zeros converging to zero or diverging to infinity.
AI.method: Uses variational methods for divergence form operators to prove existence results, and employs ODE arguments for both p-Laplacian and uniformly elliptic operators in non-divergence form to establish nonexistence results.
AI.result: Establishes ranges of parameter λ for which infinitely many small or large solutions exist, as well as ranges where no bifurcation from zero or infinity can occur, under growth conditions on the primitive F(s).
AI.conclusion: The paper provides sharp conditions for multiplicity of nonnegative solutions to p-Laplacian equations with oscillatory nonlinearities, combining variational techniques for existence with ODE methods for nonexistence across different operator classes.

title: A unified treatment of degenerate nonlocal elliptic problems
summary: We develop a unified framework for a broad class of nonlocal elliptic problems, encompassing a wide spectrum of nonlocal terms, including the classical Kirchhoff and Carrier-type equations as particular cases, and nonlinearities having sublinear or asymptotically linear growth. By combining the study of a suitable auxiliary problem and fixed-point techniques with careful parameter analysis, we establish existence, non-existence, and multiplicity results for positive solutions. Our method reveals sharp parameter thresholds and provides a comprehensive description of the solution set. Finally, for powerlike nonlinearities (including superlinear and singular ones) we provide a more direct approach, based on homogeneity.

AI.tldr: A unified framework for nonlocal elliptic problems with various nonlocal terms and nonlinearities, establishing existence, non-existence, and multiplicity results for positive solutions with sharp parameter thresholds.
AI.motivation: To develop a comprehensive theoretical framework that can handle a broad class of nonlocal elliptic problems, including classical Kirchhoff and Carrier-type equations, with various types of nonlinearities (sublinear, asymptotically linear, superlinear, singular).
AI.method: Combines study of suitable auxiliary problems with fixed-point techniques and careful parameter analysis. For powerlike nonlinearities, uses a more direct approach based on homogeneity.
AI.result: Establishes existence, non-existence, and multiplicity results for positive solutions. Reveals sharp parameter thresholds and provides comprehensive description of solution sets.
AI.conclusion: Develops a unified framework that successfully handles diverse nonlocal elliptic problems, providing complete characterization of solution behavior across different parameter regimes and nonlinearity types.

title: Global well-posedness of solutions for the equations modelling the motion of a rigid body in a bidimensional perfect fluid
summary: This paper considers a system modelling the evolution of a rigid body immersed in a bidimensional incompressible perfect fluid. In the special case of a disk-shaped rigid body, it was shown by C. Rosier and L. Rosier (2009) that the system admits a unique global solution when the initial fluid velocity $u_0$ belongs to $H^s$ ($s \ge 3$) and its vorticity $\operatorname{curl} u_0$ lies in $L^p$ with $1 \le p < 2$. By establishing a Beale-Kato-Majda type bound, we generalize the result by removing the constraint $\operatorname{curl} u_0 \in L^p$ and allowing the rigid body to be of arbitrary shape. Moreover, we obtain an explicit energy bound.

AI.tldr: Generalizes previous results on rigid body-fluid interaction by removing vorticity constraints and allowing arbitrary body shapes, establishing global existence with explicit energy bounds.
AI.motivation: Previous work by Rosier & Rosier (2009) established global existence for disk-shaped rigid bodies in 2D incompressible perfect fluids with vorticity constraints. This paper aims to generalize those results by removing the vorticity constraint and extending to arbitrary body shapes.
AI.method: Establishes a Beale-Kato-Majda type bound to prove global existence without requiring the vorticity to lie in L^p spaces. The approach works for rigid bodies of arbitrary shape rather than just disk-shaped bodies.
AI.result: Proves global existence of solutions when initial fluid velocity belongs to H^s (s ≥ 3) without requiring curl u₀ ∈ L^p. The result holds for arbitrary-shaped rigid bodies and includes an explicit energy bound.
AI.conclusion: Successfully generalizes previous results by removing the vorticity constraint and extending to arbitrary body shapes, providing a more comprehensive existence theory for rigid body-fluid interaction systems with explicit energy estimates.

title: Hausdorff measure of the free boundary for the $p$-obstacle problem with subcritical exponents

AI.tldr: The paper studies p-obstacle problems with subcritical exponents, establishing existence of non-negative weak solutions, regularity properties, and free boundary analysis including local porosity and finite Hausdorff measure.
AI.motivation: To investigate a class of p-obstacle problems with subcritical exponents, which arise in various applications involving free boundary problems and nonlinear partial differential equations with obstacle-type constraints.
AI.method: Combines mountain-pass lemma with penalty method to establish existence of weak solutions, uses De Giorgi-Nash iteration for regularity analysis, and employs Lebesgue measure estimates for gradient level sets to study free boundary properties.
AI.result: Proves existence of non-negative weak solutions, establishes L^∞ bounds and local C^{1,α} continuity, demonstrates local porosity of free boundary, and shows at least one solution has free boundary with locally finite (N-1)-dimensional Hausdorff measure.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides comprehensive analysis of p-obstacle problems with subcritical exponents, establishing existence, regularity, and free boundary properties including finite Hausdorff measure of the free boundary for at least one solution.

title: Exponential Decay for a Boundary-Controlled Nonlinear Parabolic Reactor Model
summary: We study an axial dispersion tubular reactor model governed by a nonlinear parabolic equation with Robin-type boundary conditions and boundary feedback control. We derive sufficient conditions for the exponential stability of the steady-state solution of the closed-loop system and provide an explicit estimate of the decay rate. In addition, numerical simulations are presented to illustrate the sharpness of the obtained decay rate for different choices of the feedback gain parameter.

AI.tldr: Analysis of exponential stability conditions and decay rate estimates for an axial dispersion tubular reactor with boundary feedback control.
AI.motivation: To study stability properties of axial dispersion tubular reactors with nonlinear dynamics and boundary feedback control, which are important for industrial chemical processes requiring stable operation.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis of a nonlinear parabolic equation with Robin-type boundary conditions and boundary feedback control, deriving sufficient conditions for exponential stability and explicit decay rate estimates, supplemented by numerical simulations.
AI.result: Sufficient conditions for exponential stability of the steady-state solution are established, with explicit decay rate estimates. Numerical simulations demonstrate the sharpness of these estimates for different feedback gain parameters.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides rigorous stability analysis and practical decay rate estimates for axial dispersion tubular reactors with boundary feedback control, validated by numerical simulations showing the relationship between feedback gain and stability performance.

title: A Variational link between the Olech-Opial inequality, the Wirtinger inequality, and Emden-Fowler equations
summary: We establish a structural connection between the classical Olech-Opial inequality and the Wirtinger inequality. Using an integral identity involving the mixed energy term $uu'$, we derive a nonlinear interpolation inequality linking these two results. The optimal constant is characterized by a variational problem whose extremals satisfy an Emden-Fowler equation. An explicit expression of the optimal constant is obtained in terms of the Beta function. This approach provides a natural bridge between mixed-energy integral inequalities, classical spectral estimates, and nonlinear boundary value problems.

AI.tldr: Connects Olech-Opial and Wirtinger inequalities via mixed-energy integral identity, obtains optimal constant expressed with Beta function.
AI.motivation: Establish structural relationship between classical Olech-Opial inequality and Wirtinger inequality, bridging mixed-energy integrals, spectral estimates, and nonlinear boundary problems.
AI.method: Uses integral identity involving mixed energy term uu' to derive nonlinear interpolation inequality, characterizes optimal constant via variational problem with Emden-Fowler equation extremals.
AI.result: Obtains explicit expression for optimal constant in terms of Beta function, provides natural bridge between mixed-energy integral inequalities and classical spectral estimates.
AI.conclusion: Successfully connects two classical inequalities through mixed-energy approach, yielding explicit optimal constant and linking to nonlinear boundary value problems.

title: Orbit-Level Stretching in Cubic Fourier-Galerkin Navier-Stokes: Sharp Incidence, Spectral Decay, and a Continuation Criterion
summary: I study orbit-level enstrophy stretching in a cubic Fourier-Galerkin truncation of the three-dimensional incompressible Navier-Stokes equations, reduced by the full octahedral symmetry group $O_h$. The nonlinear transfer compresses to an orbit-level matrix whose symmetric part $V_N$ governs net enstrophy growth. I reduce the stretching problem to an orbit--triad incidence estimate and close it by a face-normalized decomposition and a two-squares argument, establishing the sharp bound \begin{equation} c\,N^3\le\max_α\sum_β\sqrt{Γ_{αβ}}\le C\,N^{3}. \end{equation} A weighted-incidence refinement then yields, in the isotropic unit-energy ensemble, \begin{equation} \mathbb{E}\,ρ(V_N)\le C\, N^{-3/2}\to 0, \qquad \mathbb{E}\,ν_c^*(N)\le C\, N^{-7/2}\to 0, \end{equation} where $ν_c^*(N)=ρ(V_N)/N^2$ is the orbit-level critical-threshold ratio. For Sobolev-class data with ${ \left\lVert u \right\rVert}_{H^s}\le M$ and $s>2$, a stronger deterministic bound ${{ \left\lVert V_N \right\rVert} }_\infty\le C_s M^3$ holds uniformly in $N$, with $ν_c^*(N)\to 0$ for all $s>3/2$. A comparison with Tao's averaged Navier-Stokes construction shows that the orbit-level subcriticality is a structural property of the true nonlinearity that is violated by known blowup mechanisms. Monte Carlo experiments at $N=1,\ldots,8$ under both isotropic and Kolmogorov-spectrum ensembles confirm the decay, with $ρ(V_N)\sim N^{-2.6}$ far exceeding the proven upper bound. Tracking these bounds along the Galerkin evolution yields an orbit-level continuation criterion for the strong solution: global regularity holds if and only if $\int_0^T { \left\lVert V_N \right\rVert}_\infty\,dt$ remains bounded uniformly in $N$.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes orbit-level enstrophy stretching in 3D Navier-Stokes equations using symmetry reduction, establishing sharp bounds on nonlinear transfer and proving orbit-level subcriticality with decaying critical viscosity thresholds.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental structure of nonlinear energy transfer in 3D turbulence by studying enstrophy stretching at the orbit level under octahedral symmetry, aiming to establish rigorous bounds that reveal structural properties preventing finite-time blowup.
AI.method: Uses cubic Fourier-Galerkin truncation of 3D incompressible Navier-Stokes equations reduced by full octahedral symmetry group O_h. Develops orbit-level matrix representation of nonlinear transfer, employs orbit-triad incidence estimates with face-normalized decomposition and two-squares argument, and applies weighted-incidence refinement for statistical bounds.
AI.result: Proves sharp bound cN³ ≤ max_α∑_β√Γ_{αβ} ≤ CN³. Shows orbit-level critical viscosity ratio decays: 𝔼ν_c*(N) ≤ CN^{-7/2} → 0. For Sobolev data with s>2, gets deterministic bound ∥V_N∥_∞ ≤ C_sM³ uniformly in N. Monte Carlo experiments confirm decay with ρ(V_N) ∼ N^{-2.6}. Establishes orbit-level continuation criterion for global regularity.
AI.conclusion: Orbit-level subcriticality is a structural property of the true Navier-Stokes nonlinearity violated by known blowup mechanisms. The analysis provides rigorous bounds showing decaying critical viscosity thresholds and establishes a continuation criterion linking bounded orbit-level stretching to global regularity.

title: Nonlinear Dynamics and Performance Optimization Based on Primary Resonance of an Electromechanically Coupled Magnetic Levitation Energy Harvester
summary: This research paper explores the potential of nonlinear magnetic levitation systems for energy harvesting by developing a modified system that incorporates a more realistic energy harvesting circuit, enabling a better representation of practical operating conditions. Methodologically, approximate solutions for the system dynamics were obtained using the method of multiple scales, complemented by numerical simulations to capture parameter variations visualized through phase planes and parameter variation plots. The results demonstrate that by adjusting capacitance to induce internal and primary resonances, an extended detuning formulation (utilizing parameters sigma3 and sigma4) is innovatively introduced to capture the coupled dynamic interaction between the harvesting circuit and the mechanical system under diverse conditions. Periodic variations in circuit charge and intermediate magnet dis placement were thoroughly analyzed. Comparisons with legacy models demonstrate that the proposed coupling mechanism effectively suppresses undesirable nonlinear behaviors, such as chaos and multi-stability, resulting in a more stable and predictable energy harvesting process. Ultimately, a critical trade-off between energy harvesting efficiency and dynamical stability is identified, providing a valuable new design perspective for practical energy harvesting systems.

AI.tldr: A modified nonlinear magnetic levitation system with realistic energy harvesting circuit is developed, using method of multiple scales and numerical simulations to analyze dynamics, showing that proper capacitance adjustment suppresses chaos and improves stability while revealing efficiency-stability trade-offs.
AI.motivation: To develop a more realistic nonlinear magnetic levitation energy harvesting system that better represents practical operating conditions, addressing limitations of legacy models that don't fully capture circuit-mechanical interactions.
AI.method: Used method of multiple scales to obtain approximate solutions for system dynamics, complemented by numerical simulations with parameter variations visualized through phase planes and parameter variation plots. Introduced extended detuning formulation (sigma3 and sigma4 parameters) to capture coupled dynamic interactions.
AI.result: Adjusting capacitance to induce internal and primary resonances effectively suppresses undesirable nonlinear behaviors (chaos and multi-stability), resulting in more stable and predictable energy harvesting. Periodic variations in circuit charge and magnet displacement were analyzed, showing the proposed coupling mechanism outperforms legacy models.
AI.conclusion: Identifies critical trade-off between energy harvesting efficiency and dynamical stability, providing valuable new design perspective for practical energy harvesting systems. The extended detuning formulation successfully captures coupled dynamics between harvesting circuit and mechanical system.

title: Kruskal-style algorithm for cubic Schrödinger equation molecule reduction
summary: We are interested in the molecule reduction algorithm introduced by Deng and Hani in arXiv:2104.11204. In this article, the authors use this algorithm to establish a rigidity theorem, which plays a central role in the kinetic-time derivation of the wave equation associated with the cubic Schrödinger equation. In the present article, we show that this algorithm is a graph traversal algorithm of Kruskal type, and we prove that it constructs a Kruskal spanning tree of the input molecule. This reveals the origin of the main tool for deriving kinetic equations which has also been used for the long time derivation of the Boltzmann equation.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that the molecule reduction algorithm used in kinetic equation derivations is actually a Kruskal-type graph traversal algorithm that constructs Kruskal spanning trees.
AI.motivation: To understand the mathematical structure and origin of the molecule reduction algorithm introduced by Deng and Hani, which has been used as a key tool for deriving kinetic equations like the wave equation for cubic Schrödinger equation and the Boltzmann equation.
AI.method: Analyze the molecule reduction algorithm as a graph traversal algorithm, specifically identifying it as a Kruskal-type algorithm, and prove that it constructs Kruskal spanning trees of input molecules.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that the molecule reduction algorithm is equivalent to a Kruskal graph traversal algorithm and proves it constructs Kruskal spanning trees, revealing the underlying graph-theoretic structure of this important mathematical tool.
AI.conclusion: The molecule reduction algorithm used in kinetic equation derivations has a fundamental graph-theoretic interpretation as a Kruskal spanning tree construction algorithm, explaining its mathematical origin and structure.

title: Energy-Morawetz estimates for Teukolsky equations in perturbations of Kerr
comment: 247 pages, 2 figures, a companion paper of arXiv:2410.02341
summary: In this paper, we prove energy and Morawetz estimates for solutions to Teukolsky equations in spacetimes with metrics that are perturbations, compatible with nonlinear applications, of Kerr metrics in the full subextremal range. The Teukolsky equations are written in tensorial form using the non-integrable formalism in \cite{GKS22}, and we follow the approach in \cite{Ma} of relying on a Teukolsky wave/transport system. The estimates are proved by extending the ideas from our earlier result \cite{MaSz24} on the corresponding problem for the scalar wave, notably the use of $r$-foliation-adapted microlocal multipliers for the wave part, and by incorporating techniques from \cite{Ma} to control the linear coupling terms between the components of the Teukolsky wave/transport system. Additionally, in order to adapt the methodology of \cite{MaSz24} to tensorial waves, we introduce a well-suited regular scalarization procedure which is of independent interest. This result, alongside our companion paper \cite{MaSz24}, is an essential step towards extending the current proof of Kerr stability in \cite{GCM1} \cite{GCM2} \cite{KS:Kerr} \cite{GKS22} \cite{Shen}, valid in the slowly rotating case, to a complete resolution of the Kerr stability conjecture, i.e., the statement that the Kerr family of spacetimes is nonlinearly stable for all subextremal angular momenta.

AI.tldr: Proves energy and Morawetz estimates for Teukolsky equations in perturbed Kerr spacetimes, extending scalar wave methods to tensorial case using wave/transport system approach.
AI.motivation: To extend the proof of Kerr stability from slowly rotating cases to the full subextremal range, addressing the Kerr stability conjecture for all subextremal angular momenta.
AI.method: Uses Teukolsky equations in tensorial form with non-integrable formalism, follows wave/transport system approach, extends r-foliation-adapted microlocal multipliers from scalar wave case, introduces regular scalarization procedure for tensorial waves, and controls linear coupling terms.
AI.result: Successfully proves energy and Morawetz estimates for Teukolsky equations in perturbed Kerr metrics in full subextremal range, providing essential step towards complete resolution of Kerr stability conjecture.
AI.conclusion: This work, alongside companion paper, represents crucial progress toward proving nonlinear stability of Kerr family for all subextremal angular momenta, extending current slowly rotating results to full subextremal range.

title: Double Coupling Architecture and Training Method for Optimization Problems of Differential Algebraic Equations with Parameters
summary: Simulation and modeling are essential in product development, integrated into the design and manufacturing process to enhance efficiency and quality. They are typically represented as complex nonlinear differential algebraic equations. The growing diversity of product requirements demands multi-task optimization, a key challenge in simulation modeling research. A dual physics-informed neural network architecture has been proposed to decouple constraints and objective functions in parametric differential algebraic equation optimization problems. Theoretical analysis shows that introducing a relaxation variable with a global error bound ensures solution equivalence between the network and optimization problem. A genetic algorithm-enhanced training framework for physics-informed neural networks improves training precision and efficiency, avoiding redundant solving of differential algebraic equations. This approach enables generalization for multi-task objectives with a single, training maintaining real-time responsiveness to product requirements.

AI.tldr: A dual physics-informed neural network architecture with genetic algorithm enhancement for multi-task optimization of parametric differential algebraic equations in product development.
AI.motivation: Product development requires efficient simulation modeling with multi-task optimization capabilities, but traditional approaches face challenges with complex nonlinear differential algebraic equations and diverse product requirements.
AI.method: Dual physics-informed neural network architecture decouples constraints and objective functions, uses relaxation variables with global error bounds, and employs genetic algorithm-enhanced training framework to avoid redundant solving of differential algebraic equations.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows solution equivalence between network and optimization problem, with improved training precision and efficiency. The approach enables generalization for multi-task objectives with single training while maintaining real-time responsiveness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture effectively addresses multi-task optimization challenges in simulation modeling for product development, providing efficient and precise solutions for parametric differential algebraic equation optimization problems.

title: Eigenvalue stability of Hermitian and normal matrices
summary: The ordered eigenvalues define a Lipschitz map on the real vector space of Hermitian $d \times d$ matrices. We prove that this map acts continuously, but not uniformly continuously, by superposition on the Sobolev spaces $W^{1,q}$, for all $1 \le q < \infty$, on bounded open domains. For $q=\infty$, the action is still well-defined and bounded but not continuous. We show that this stability result extends to normal matrices, where the eigenvalues are naturally interpreted as multivalued Sobolev functions in the sense of Almgren. Several applications are given, including the stability of singular values, condition numbers of matrices, surface area of eigenvalue graphs, and compact self-adjoint operators in Hilbert space.

AI.tldr: Eigenvalue map from Hermitian matrices to ordered eigenvalues is Lipschitz continuous but not uniformly continuous on Sobolev spaces W^{1,q} for 1≤q<∞, and bounded but not continuous for q=∞. Results extend to normal matrices with eigenvalues as multivalued Sobolev functions.
AI.motivation: To understand the regularity properties of eigenvalue maps when applied to matrix-valued functions, particularly in Sobolev spaces, which is important for applications involving matrix perturbations, stability analysis, and spectral theory in functional analysis.
AI.method: Analyze the ordered eigenvalue map as a Lipschitz function on Hermitian matrices, study its superposition properties on Sobolev spaces W^{1,q} for different q values, extend analysis to normal matrices using Almgren's theory of multivalued Sobolev functions.
AI.result: The eigenvalue map acts continuously (but not uniformly continuously) on W^{1,q} for 1≤q<∞, and is bounded but not continuous on W^{∞}. Results generalize to normal matrices with eigenvalues as multivalued Sobolev functions.
AI.conclusion: Eigenvalue maps preserve Sobolev regularity with important stability implications for applications including singular values, condition numbers, eigenvalue graph surface area, and compact self-adjoint operators in Hilbert space.

title: Maximal growth of the Stein-Wainger oscillatory integral
summary: We establish a precise hierarchy for the maximal growth of the Stein-Wainger oscillatory integral as the regularity of the phase varies over Denjoy-Carleman classes, such as the Gevrey classes and their generalizations. In particular, we resolve a problem posed by Wang--Zhang, motivated by eigenfunction restriction estimates on curves, and also provide a new proof of a theorem of Nagel--Wainger on the Hilbert transform along curves. A key ingredient is the sharp estimate on the growth of a phase near a flat point.

AI.tldr: Establishes precise hierarchy for maximal growth of Stein-Wainger oscillatory integral across Denjoy-Carleman classes, resolving Wang-Zhang problem and providing new proof of Nagel-Wainger theorem.
AI.motivation: Motivated by eigenfunction restriction estimates on curves and understanding the growth behavior of oscillatory integrals as phase regularity varies across different function classes.
AI.method: Analyzes maximal growth of Stein-Wainger oscillatory integral across Denjoy-Carleman classes (including Gevrey classes), with key ingredient being sharp estimates on phase growth near flat points.
AI.result: Establishes precise hierarchy for growth rates, resolves problem posed by Wang-Zhang, and provides new proof of Nagel-Wainger theorem on Hilbert transform along curves.
AI.conclusion: Provides comprehensive understanding of oscillatory integral growth across regularity classes, connecting to eigenfunction restriction estimates and Hilbert transform theory.

title: Solutions of the constraints with controlled decay to Kerr, including Schwartz decay
summary: We show that to every small and decaying solution of the linearized constraint equations about Minkowski spacetime, one can add a quadratically small correction to obtain a solution of the full constraint equations. Near spacelike infinity, the correction is given by Kerr black hole initial data, up to a term that decays faster than the linearized solution, and that has Schwartz decay if the linearized solution has Schwartz decay. Using a recent result, we obtain that the solutions of the Einstein equations with these initial data admit a regular conformal compactification along null and timelike infinity. The construction is based on a right inverse (up to necessary integrability conditions) for the linearized constraint operator about Minkowski initial data obtained previously, that has optimal mapping properties relative to weighted b-Sobolev spaces, where the weights measure decay towards infinity. On an algebraic level, we show that the constraint equations can be derived using the homotopy transfer theorem, rather than using the geometric Gauss and Codazzi equations.

AI.tldr: The paper constructs solutions to Einstein's constraint equations near Minkowski spacetime by adding quadratically small corrections to linearized solutions, with the corrections approaching Kerr black hole data near infinity.
AI.motivation: To construct physically relevant initial data for Einstein's equations that approach Minkowski spacetime at infinity, particularly solutions that can be conformally compactified and have proper asymptotic behavior.
AI.method: Uses a right inverse for the linearized constraint operator with optimal mapping properties in weighted b-Sobolev spaces, applies homotopy transfer theorem for algebraic derivation of constraint equations, and constructs corrections to linearized solutions.
AI.result: Shows that small decaying linearized solutions can be corrected to full constraint solutions, with corrections approaching Kerr black hole data near infinity, and resulting Einstein solutions admit regular conformal compactification.
AI.conclusion: Provides a systematic method to construct initial data for Einstein's equations with proper asymptotic behavior, connecting linearized solutions to physically relevant black hole data and enabling conformal compactification analysis.
